**Jamaica**[[a]](#cite_note-8) is an [island country](/wiki/Island_country "Island country") in the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean"), covering 10,990 square kilometres (4,240 sq mi). It is the third-largest island in the [Greater Antilles](/wiki/Greater_Antilles "Greater Antilles") and the Caribbean, after [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba") and the island of [Hispaniola](/wiki/Hispaniola "Hispaniola").[[8]](#cite_note-CIA_World_Factbook_–_Jamaica-9) Jamaica lies about 145 km (78 nmi) south of Cuba, 191 km (103 nmi) west of Hispaniola (the island containing [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti") and the [Dominican Republic](/wiki/Dominican_Republic "Dominican Republic")), and 215 km (116 nmi) southeast of the [Cayman Islands](/wiki/Cayman_Islands "Cayman Islands") (a [British Overseas Territory](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territories "British Overseas Territories")).[[8]](#cite_note-CIA_World_Factbook_–_Jamaica-9) With 2.8 million people,[[9]](#cite_note-UN_WPP-10)[[10]](#cite_note-UN_WPP_2022-11) Jamaica is the third most populous [Anglophone](/wiki/English-speaking_world "English-speaking world") country in the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas") and the fourth most populous country in the Caribbean. [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_Jamaica "Kingston, Jamaica") is the country's capital and largest city.

The indigenous [Taíno](/wiki/Ta%C3%ADno "Taíno") peoples of the island gradually came under [Spanish rule](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") after the arrival of [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus") in 1494. Many of the indigenous people either were killed or died of diseases, after which the Spanish brought large numbers of Africans to Jamaica as slaves.[[8]](#cite_note-CIA_World_Factbook_–_Jamaica-9) The island remained a possession of Spain, under the name [Santiago](/wiki/Colony_of_Santiago "Colony of Santiago"), until 1655, when [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") (part of what would become the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain")) conquered it and named it *Jamaica*. It became an important part of the colonial [British West Indies](/wiki/British_West_Indies "British West Indies"). Under Britain's colonial rule, Jamaica became a leading sugar exporter, with a plantation economy dependent on continued importation of African slaves and their descendants. The British fully emancipated all slaves in 1838, and many freedmen chose to have [subsistence farms](/wiki/Subsistence_agriculture "Subsistence agriculture") rather than to work on plantations. Beginning in the 1840s, the British began using [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_people "Chinese people") and [Indian](/wiki/Indian_people "Indian people") [indentured](/wiki/Indenture "Indenture") labourers for plantation work. Jamaicans achieved independence from the United Kingdom on 6 August 1962.[[8]](#cite_note-CIA_World_Factbook_–_Jamaica-9)

Jamaica is a parliamentary [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy"), with power vested in the bicameral [Parliament of Jamaica](/wiki/Parliament_of_Jamaica "Parliament of Jamaica"), consisting of an appointed Senate and a directly elected House of Representatives.[[8]](#cite_note-CIA_World_Factbook_–_Jamaica-9) [Andrew Holness](/wiki/Andrew_Holness "Andrew Holness") has served as [Prime Minister of Jamaica](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Jamaica "Prime Minister of Jamaica") since March 2016. Jamaica is a [Commonwealth realm](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm"), with [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") as [its king](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Jamaica "Monarchy of Jamaica"); the appointed representative of the Crown is the [Governor-General of Jamaica](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Jamaica "Governor-General of Jamaica"), the office having been held by [Patrick Allen](/wiki/Patrick_Allen_(governor-general) "Patrick Allen (governor-general)") since 2009. Because of a high rate of emigration for work since the 1960s, there is a large [Jamaican diaspora](/wiki/Jamaican_diaspora "Jamaican diaspora"), particularly in Canada, the United Kingdom, and the United States. Most [Jamaicans](/wiki/Jamaicans "Jamaicans") are of [Sub-Saharan African](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa") ancestry, with significant [European](/wiki/White_Jamaicans "White Jamaicans"), [East Asian](/wiki/East_Asian_people "East Asian people") (primarily [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_Jamaicans "Chinese Jamaicans")), [Indian](/wiki/Indo-Jamaicans "Indo-Jamaicans"), [Lebanese](/wiki/Lebanese_Jamaicans "Lebanese Jamaicans"), and mixed-race minorities.[[8]](#cite_note-CIA_World_Factbook_–_Jamaica-9)

Jamaica is an upper-middle-income country[[11]](#cite_note-Jamaica_country-12) with an economy heavily dependent on [tourism](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism"), including popular [resorts](/wiki/Resort "Resort"). It has an average of 4.3 million tourists a year.[[12]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-13) The country has a global influence that belies its small size; it is the birthplace of the [Rastafari](/wiki/Rastafari "Rastafari") religion and [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae") music, along with the associated musical genres of [dub](/wiki/Dub_music "Dub music"), [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska"), and [dancehall](/wiki/Dancehall "Dancehall"); and it is internationally prominent in sports, including [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"), [sprinting](/wiki/Sprint_(running) "Sprint (running)"), and [athletics](/wiki/Sport_of_athletics "Sport of athletics").[[13]](#cite_note-track-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15)[[15]](#cite_note-state2007-16)[[11]](#cite_note-Jamaica_country-12) Jamaica has sometimes been considered the world's least populous [cultural superpower](/wiki/Power_(international_relations)#Other_categories "Power (international relations)").[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18)[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20)

## Etymology

The [indigenous people](/wiki/Indigenous_people "Indigenous people"), the Taíno, called the island *Xaymaca* in their [language](/wiki/Ta%C3%ADno_language "Taíno language"),[[b]](#cite_note-21) meaning the "Land of Wood and Water" or the "Land of Springs".[[20]](#cite_note-taino-22) *Yamaye* has been suggested as an early Taíno name for the island as recorded by [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus").[[21]](#cite_note-Atkinson-23)

Jamaicans often refer to their home island as "yaad" – the Jamaican Patois word for yard, meaning home. Other colloquial names such as "Jamrock", "Jamdown" ("Jamdung" in [Jamaican Patois](/wiki/Jamaican_Patois "Jamaican Patois")), or briefly "Ja" are also widely used.[[22]](#cite_note-24)[*[user-generated source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:UGC "Wikipedia:UGC")*][[23]](#cite_note-CIA_World_Factbook_–_Jamaica2-25)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

## History

Main article: [History of Jamaica](/wiki/History_of_Jamaica "History of Jamaica")

### Prehistory

Main article: [Pre-Columbian Jamaica](/wiki/Pre-Columbian_Jamaica "Pre-Columbian Jamaica")

There is no archaeological evidence of any human presence on Jamaica until about 500.[[24]](#cite_note-26) A group known as the "Redware people" after their pottery arrived circa 600,[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27) followed by the Taíno circa 800, who most likely came from [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America").[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27)[[26]](#cite_note-28) They practised an agrarian and fishing economy, and at their height are thought to have numbered some 60,000 people, grouped into around 200 villages headed by *[caciques](/wiki/Cacique "Cacique")* (chiefs).[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27) The south coast of Jamaica was the most populated, especially around the area now known as Old Harbour.[[27]](#cite_note-primearticles1-29)

Though often thought to have become extinct following contact with Europeans, the Taíno in fact still inhabited Jamaica when the English took control of the island in 1655.[[27]](#cite_note-primearticles1-29) Some fled into interior regions, merging with African [Maroon](/wiki/Jamaican_Maroons "Jamaican Maroons") communities.[[28]](#cite_note-30)[[29]](#cite_note-31)[[30]](#cite_note-32) The Jamaican National Heritage Trust is attempting to locate and document any remaining evidence of the Taíno.[[31]](#cite_note-33)

### Spanish rule (1509–1655)

Main article: [Colony of Santiago](/wiki/Colony_of_Santiago "Colony of Santiago")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Cristobal_Columbus20231003_115330.jpg/250px-Cristobal_Columbus20231003_115330.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cristobal_Columbus20231003_115330.jpg)

Statue of [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus") in [St. Ann Parish](/wiki/St._Ann_Parish "St. Ann Parish")

Christopher Columbus was the first European to see Jamaica, claiming the island for Spain after landing there in 1494 on his second voyage to the Americas.[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27) His probable landing point was Dry Harbour, called [Discovery Bay](/wiki/Discovery_Bay,_Jamaica "Discovery Bay, Jamaica"),[[32]](#cite_note-columbus-34) and [St. Ann's Bay](/wiki/Saint_Ann_Parish "Saint Ann Parish") was named "Saint Gloria" by Columbus, as the first sighting of the land. He later returned in 1503; however, he was shipwrecked and he and his crew were forced to live on Jamaica for a year while waiting to be rescued.[[33]](#cite_note-35)

One and a half kilometres west of St. Ann's Bay is the site of the first Spanish settlement on the island, [Sevilla](/wiki/Sevilla_la_Nueva_(Jamaica) "Sevilla la Nueva (Jamaica)"), which was established in 1509 by [Juan de Esquivel](/wiki/Juan_de_Esquivel "Juan de Esquivel") but abandoned around 1524 because it was deemed unhealthy.[[34]](#cite_note-JNHT-36) The capital was moved to [Spanish Town](/wiki/Spanish_Town "Spanish Town"), then called *St. Jago de la Vega*, around 1534.[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27)[[35]](#cite_note-Spanish_town-37) Meanwhile, the Taínos began dying in large numbers, both from introduced diseases and from enslavement by the Spanish.[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27) As a result, the Spanish began importing slaves from Africa to the island.[[36]](#cite_note-discja-38)

Many slaves managed to escape, forming autonomous communities in remote and easily defended areas in the interior of Jamaica, mixing with the remaining Taino; these communities became known as [Maroons](/wiki/Jamaican_Maroons "Jamaican Maroons").[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27) [Many Jews fled the Spanish Inquisition](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Jamaica "History of the Jews in Jamaica") to live on the island.[[37]](#cite_note-39) They lived as [conversos](/wiki/Conversos "Conversos") and were often persecuted by the Spanish rulers, and some turned to [piracy against the Spanish Empire's shipping](/wiki/Jewish_pirates "Jewish pirates").[[38]](#cite_note-40)

By the early 17th century it is estimated that no more than 2,500–3,000 people lived on Jamaica.[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27)[[39]](#cite_note-41)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

### Early British period

Main article: [Colony of Jamaica](/wiki/Colony_of_Jamaica "Colony of Jamaica")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Morgan%2CHenry.jpg/250px-Morgan%2CHenry.jpg)](/wiki/File:Morgan,Henry.jpg)

[Henry Morgan](/wiki/Henry_Morgan "Henry Morgan") was a [Caribbean pirate](/wiki/Piracy_in_the_Caribbean "Piracy in the Caribbean"), privateer, plantation owner and slaveholder; he had first come to the West Indies as an indentured servant, like most of the early English colonists.[[40]](#cite_note-42)

The English began taking an interest in the island and, following a failed attempt to conquer [Santo Domingo](/wiki/Captaincy_General_of_Santo_Domingo "Captaincy General of Santo Domingo") on [Hispaniola](/wiki/Hispaniola "Hispaniola"), [Admiral William Penn](/wiki/William_Penn_(Royal_Navy_officer) "William Penn (Royal Navy officer)") and General [Robert Venables](/wiki/Robert_Venables "Robert Venables") led an [invasion of Jamaica](/wiki/Invasion_of_Jamaica "Invasion of Jamaica") in 1655.[[41]](#cite_note-43) Battles at [Ocho Rios in 1657](/wiki/Battle_of_Ocho_Rios "Battle of Ocho Rios") and the [Rio Nuevo in 1658](/wiki/Battle_of_Rio_Nuevo "Battle of Rio Nuevo") resulted in Spanish defeats; in 1660 the Maroon community under the leadership of [Juan de Bolas](/wiki/Juan_de_Bolas "Juan de Bolas") switched sides from the Spanish, and began supporting the English. With their help, the Spanish defeat was secured.[[42]](#cite_note-jamsenghist-44) In 1661 English civil government was formed and [Roundhead](/wiki/Roundhead "Roundhead") soldiers turned their attention to governance and agricultural responsibilities.[[43]](#cite_note-45)

When the English captured Jamaica, most Spanish colonists fled, with the exception of Spanish Jews, who chose to remain. Spanish slave holders freed their slaves before leaving.[[42]](#cite_note-jamsenghist-44) Many slaves dispersed into the mountains, joining the already established [maroon](/wiki/Maroon_(people) "Maroon (people)") communities.[[44]](#cite_note-maroons-46) During the centuries of slavery, [Jamaican Maroons](/wiki/Jamaican_Maroons "Jamaican Maroons") established free communities in the mountainous interior of Jamaica, where they maintained their freedom and independence for generations, under the leadership of Maroon leaders such as [Juan de Serras](/wiki/Juan_de_Serras "Juan de Serras").[[45]](#cite_note-47)

Meanwhile, the Spanish made several attempts to re-capture the island, prompting the British to support pirates attacking Spanish ships in the Caribbean; as a result piracy became rampant on Jamaica, with the city of [Port Royal](/wiki/Port_Royal "Port Royal") becoming notorious for its lawlessness. Spain later recognised English possession of the island with the [Treaty of Madrid (1670)](/wiki/Treaty_of_Madrid_(1670) "Treaty of Madrid (1670)").[[46]](#cite_note-48) After that, the English authorities sought to rein in the worst excesses of the pirates.[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27) During this period, Cromwell had begun to transport Scottish prisoners of war from [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") to the island, with 1,200 Scots being sent to the island which had recently been colonised by England. The method of sending Scottish prisoners of war to the island continued, and as a result, Scots played a significant role in the development of the island, being credited as "indentured and skilled labourers".[[47]](#cite_note-adventuresfromelle.com-49)

In 1660, the population of Jamaica was about 4,500 white and 1,500 black.[[48]](#cite_note-population-50) By the early 1670s, as the English developed sugar cane plantations worked by large numbers of slaves, black Africans formed a majority of the population.[[49]](#cite_note-51) The [Irish in Jamaica](/wiki/Irish_people_in_Jamaica "Irish people in Jamaica") also formed a large part of the island's early population, making up two-thirds of the white population on the island in the late 17th century, twice that of the English population. They were brought in as indentured labourers and soldiers after the conquest of 1655. The majority of Irish were transported by force as political prisoners of war from Ireland as a result of the ongoing [Wars of the Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "Wars of the Three Kingdoms").[[50]](#cite_note-52) Migration of large numbers of Irish to the island continued into the 18th century.[[51]](#cite_note-53)

A limited form of local government was introduced with the creation of the [House of Assembly of Jamaica](/wiki/House_of_Assembly_of_Jamaica "House of Assembly of Jamaica") in 1664; however, it represented only a tiny number of rich plantation owners.[[52]](#cite_note-HJ-54) In 1692, the colony was rocked by an [earthquake](/wiki/1692_Jamaica_earthquake "1692 Jamaica earthquake") that resulted in several thousand deaths and the almost complete destruction of Port Royal.[[53]](#cite_note-USGS-55)

### 18th–19th centuries

Main article: [Slavery in colonial British America](/wiki/Slavery_in_colonial_British_America "Slavery in colonial British America")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Duperly_%281833%29_Destruction_of_the_Roehampton_Estate_January_1832.png/250px-Duperly_%281833%29_Destruction_of_the_Roehampton_Estate_January_1832.png)](/wiki/File:Duperly_(1833)_Destruction_of_the_Roehampton_Estate_January_1832.png)

A plantation set alight during the Baptist War of 1831–32

During the 1700s the economy boomed, based largely on sugar and other crops for export such as [coffee](/wiki/Coffee "Coffee"), [cotton](/wiki/Cotton "Cotton") and [indigo](/wiki/Indigo "Indigo"). All these crops were worked by black slaves, who lived short and often brutal lives with no rights, being the property of a small planter-class.[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27) In the 18th century, slaves ran away and joined the Maroons in increasing numbers, and resulted in The [First Maroon War](/wiki/First_Maroon_War "First Maroon War") (1728 – 1739/40), which ended in stalemate. The British government sued for peace, and signed treaties with the Leeward Maroons led by [Cudjoe](/wiki/Cudjoe "Cudjoe") and [Accompong](/wiki/Accompong "Accompong") in 1739, and the Windward Maroons led by [Quao](/wiki/Quao "Quao") and [Queen Nanny](/wiki/Queen_Nanny "Queen Nanny") in 1740.[[54]](#cite_note-56)

A large slave rebellion, known as [Tacky's War](/wiki/Tacky%27s_War "Tacky's War"), broke out in 1760 but was defeated by the British and their Maroon allies.[[55]](#cite_note-jamaicans1-57) After the [second conflict](/wiki/Second_Maroon_War "Second Maroon War") in 1795–96, many Maroons from the Maroon town of [Cudjoe's Town (Trelawny Town)](/wiki/Cudjoe%27s_Town_(Trelawny_Town) "Cudjoe's Town (Trelawny Town)") were expelled to [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia") and, later, [Sierra Leone](/wiki/Sierra_Leone "Sierra Leone").[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Hakewill%2C_A_Picturesque_Tour_of_the_Island_of_Jamaica%2C_Plate_04.jpg/250px-Hakewill%2C_A_Picturesque_Tour_of_the_Island_of_Jamaica%2C_Plate_04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hakewill,_A_Picturesque_Tour_of_the_Island_of_Jamaica,_Plate_04.jpg)

Harbour Street, Kingston, c. 1820

By the beginning of the 19th century, Jamaica's dependence on slave labour and a plantation economy had resulted in black people outnumbering [white people](/wiki/White_people "White people") by a ratio of almost 20 to 1. Although the British had outlawed the importation of slaves, some were still smuggled in from Spanish colonies and directly from Africa.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] While planning the abolition of slavery, the British Parliament passed laws to improve conditions for slaves. They banned the use of whips in the field and flogging of women; informed planters that slaves were to be allowed religious instruction, and required a free day during each week when slaves could sell their produce,[[56]](#cite_note-58) prohibiting Sunday markets to enable slaves to attend church.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The House of Assembly in Jamaica resented and resisted the new laws. Members, with membership then restricted to European-descended Jamaicans, claimed that the slaves were content and objected to Parliament's interference in island affairs. Slave owners feared possible revolts if conditions were lightened.

The British [abolished the slave trade](/wiki/Slave_Trade_Act_1807 "Slave Trade Act 1807") in 1807, but not the institution itself.[[57]](#cite_note-59) In 1831 a huge slave rebellion, known as the [Baptist War](/wiki/Baptist_War "Baptist War"), broke out, led by the Baptist preacher [Samuel Sharpe](/wiki/Samuel_Sharpe "Samuel Sharpe"). The rebellion resulted in hundreds of deaths and the destruction of many plantations, and led to ferocious reprisals by the plantocracy class.[[58]](#cite_note-Revauger-60)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Proclamation_of_the_abolition_of_slavery_in_the_Colony_of_Jamaica%2C_August_1%2C_1838%2C_in_Spanish_Town_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Proclamation_of_the_abolition_of_slavery_in_the_Colony_of_Jamaica%2C_August_1%2C_1838%2C_in_Spanish_Town_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Proclamation_of_the_abolition_of_slavery_in_the_Colony_of_Jamaica,_August_1,_1838,_in_Spanish_Town_(cropped).jpg)

Governor [Sir Lionel Smith](/wiki/Sir_Lionel_Smith,_1st_Baronet "Sir Lionel Smith, 1st Baronet"), accompanied by Revd [James Phillippo](/wiki/James_Phillippo "James Phillippo"), proclaiming the [abolition of slavery](/wiki/Abolition_of_slavery_in_the_United_States "Abolition of slavery in the United States") in the colony of Jamaica, on 1 August 1838, from the King's House in [Spanish Town](/wiki/Spanish_Town "Spanish Town")

As a result of rebellions such as these, as well as the efforts of abolitionists, Britain outlawed slavery in its empire in 1834, with full [emancipation](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Abolitionism in the United Kingdom") from chattel slavery declared in 1838.[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27) The population in 1834 was 371,070, of whom 15,000 were white, 5,000 free black; 40,000 "coloured" or [free people of colour](/wiki/Free_people_of_color "Free people of color") ([mixed race](/wiki/Mixed_race "Mixed race")); and 311,070 were slaves.[[48]](#cite_note-population-50)

The resulting labour shortage prompted the British to begin to "import" [indentured servants](/wiki/Indentured_servant "Indentured servant") to supplement the labour pool, as many freedmen resisted working on the plantations.[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27) Workers recruited from India began arriving in 1845, Chinese workers in 1854.[[59]](#cite_note-61) Many Jamaicans are descendants of South Asian and Chinese people.[[60]](#cite_note-tortello-62)[[61]](#cite_note-63) Over the next 20 years, several epidemics of [cholera](/wiki/Cholera "Cholera"), [scarlet fever](/wiki/Scarlet_fever "Scarlet fever"), and [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox") hit the island, killing almost 60,000 people (about 10 per day).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Nevertheless, in 1871 the census recorded a population of 506,154 people, 246,573 of whom were males, and 259,581 females. Their races were recorded as 13,101 white, 100,346 coloured (known as the Browning Class), and 392,707 black.[[62]](#cite_note-64) There was an economic slump in this period, with many Jamaicans living in poverty. Dissatisfaction with this, and continued racial discrimination and marginalisation of the black majority, led to the outbreak of the [Morant Bay rebellion](/wiki/Morant_Bay_rebellion "Morant Bay rebellion") in 1865, led by [Paul Bogle](/wiki/Paul_Bogle "Paul Bogle"), which was put down by Governor [John Eyre](/wiki/Edward_John_Eyre "Edward John Eyre") with such brutality that he was recalled from his position.[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27) His successor, [John Peter Grant](/wiki/John_Peter_Grant "John Peter Grant"), enacted a series of social, financial and political reforms while aiming to uphold firm British rule over the island, which became a Crown Colony in 1866.[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27) In 1872 the capital was transferred from Spanish Town to Kingston.[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27)

### Early 20th century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Marcus_Garvey_1924-08-05.jpg/250px-Marcus_Garvey_1924-08-05.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marcus_Garvey_1924-08-05.jpg)

[Marcus Garvey](/wiki/Marcus_Garvey "Marcus Garvey"), father of the Back to Africa Movement and Jamaica's first National Hero

In 1907, Jamaica was struck by [an earthquake](/wiki/1907_Kingston_earthquake "1907 Kingston earthquake") which, together with the subsequent fire, resulted in considerable destruction in Kingston and caused the deaths of between 800 and 1,000 people.[[63]](#cite_note-Wilson-65)[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27)

Unemployment and poverty remained a problem for many Jamaicans. Various movements seeking political change arose as a result, most notably the [Universal Negro Improvement Association and African Communities League](/wiki/Universal_Negro_Improvement_Association_and_African_Communities_League "Universal Negro Improvement Association and African Communities League") founded by [Marcus Garvey](/wiki/Marcus_Garvey "Marcus Garvey") in 1917. As well as seeking greater political rights and an improvement for the condition of workers, Garvey was also a prominent [Pan-Africanist](/wiki/Pan-Africanist "Pan-Africanist") and proponent of the [Back-to-Africa movement](/wiki/Back-to-Africa_movement "Back-to-Africa movement").[[64]](#cite_note-back-66) He was also one of the chief inspirations behind Rastafari, a religion founded in Jamaica in the 1930s that combined Christianity with an [Afrocentric](/wiki/Afrocentrism "Afrocentrism") theology focused on the figure of [Haile Selassie](/wiki/Haile_Selassie "Haile Selassie"), Emperor of [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia"). Despite occasional persecution, Rastafari grew to become an established faith on the island, later spreading abroad.

The [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") of the 1930s hit Jamaica hard. As part of the [British West Indian labour unrest of 1934–39](/wiki/British_West_Indian_labour_unrest_of_1934%E2%80%9339 "British West Indian labour unrest of 1934–39"), Jamaica saw numerous strikes, culminating in a strike in 1938 that turned into a riot.[[65]](#cite_note-67)[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27)[[66]](#cite_note-68)
As a result, the British government instituted [a commission](/wiki/Report_of_West_India_Royal_Commission_(Moyne_Report) "Report of West India Royal Commission (Moyne Report)") to look into the causes of the disturbances; their report recommended political and economic reforms in Britain's Caribbean colonies.[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27)[[67]](#cite_note-69) A new House of Representatives was established in 1944, elected by universal adult suffrage.[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27) During this period Jamaica's two-party system emerged, with the creation of the [Jamaican Labour Party](/wiki/Jamaican_Labour_Party "Jamaican Labour Party") (JLP) under [Alexander Bustamante](/wiki/Alexander_Bustamante "Alexander Bustamante") and the [People's National Party](/wiki/People%27s_National_Party "People's National Party") (PNP) under [Norman Manley](/wiki/Norman_Manley "Norman Manley").[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27)

Jamaica slowly gained increasing autonomy from the United Kingdom. In 1958 it became a province in the [Federation of the West Indies](/wiki/Federation_of_the_West_Indies "Federation of the West Indies"), a federation of several of [Britain's Caribbean colonies](/wiki/British_West_Indies "British West Indies").[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27) Membership of the Federation proved to be divisive, however, and a [referendum](/wiki/1961_Jamaican_Federation_of_the_West_Indies_membership_referendum "1961 Jamaican Federation of the West Indies membership referendum") on the issue saw a slight majority voting to leave.[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27) After leaving the Federation, Jamaica attained full [independence](/wiki/Independence_of_Jamaica "Independence of Jamaica") on 6 August 1962.[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27) The new state retained, however, its membership in the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") (with the British monarch as head of state) and adopted a [Westminster-style parliamentary system](/wiki/Westminster_parliamentary_system "Westminster parliamentary system"). Bustamante, at the age of 78, became the country's first prime minister.[[68]](#cite_note-70)[[69]](#cite_note-71)

### Post-independence era

Main article: [Independence of Jamaica](/wiki/Independence_of_Jamaica "Independence of Jamaica")

Strong economic growth, averaging approximately 6% per year, marked the first ten years of independence under conservative JLP governments; these were led by successive Prime Ministers Alexander Bustamante, [Donald Sangster](/wiki/Donald_Sangster "Donald Sangster") (who died of natural causes within two months of taking office) and [Hugh Shearer](/wiki/Hugh_Shearer "Hugh Shearer").[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27) The growth was fuelled by high levels of private investment in quarrying [bauxite](/wiki/Bauxite "Bauxite")/[alumina](/wiki/Alumina "Alumina") ([aluminium](/wiki/Aluminium "Aluminium") ore), [tourism](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism"), the manufacturing industry and, to a lesser extent, the agricultural sector. In the [1967 Jamaican general election](/wiki/1967_Jamaican_general_election "1967 Jamaican general election"), the JLP were victorious again, winning 33 out of 53 seats, with the PNP taking 20 seats.[[70]](#cite_note-Dieter_Nohlen_2005_p._430-72)

In terms of foreign policy Jamaica became a member of the [Non-Aligned Movement](/wiki/Non-Aligned_Movement "Non-Aligned Movement"), seeking to retain strong ties with Britain and the United States whilst also developing links with Communist states such as Cuba.[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Michael_Manley.jpg/250px-Michael_Manley.jpg)](/wiki/File:Michael_Manley.jpg)

[Michael Manley](/wiki/Michael_Manley "Michael Manley"), Prime Minister 1972–1980 and 1989–1992

The optimism of the first decade was accompanied by a growing sense of inequality among many Afro-Jamaicans, and a concern that the benefits of growth were not being shared by the urban poor, many of whom ended up living in crime-ridden shanty towns in Kingston.[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27) This led to the voters [electing the PNP](/wiki/1972_Jamaican_general_election "1972 Jamaican general election") under [Michael Manley](/wiki/Michael_Manley "Michael Manley") in 1972. The PNP won 37 seats to the JLP's 16.[[70]](#cite_note-Dieter_Nohlen_2005_p._430-72)

Manley's government enacted various social reforms, such as a higher [minimum wage](/wiki/Minimum_wage "Minimum wage"), land reform, legislation for women's equality, greater housing construction and an increase in educational provision.[[71]](#cite_note-73)[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27) Internationally he improved ties with the Communist bloc and vigorously opposed the [apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid") regime in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa").[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27)

In 1976, the PNP won another landslide, winning 47 seats to the JLP's 13. The turnout was a very high 85 percent.[[72]](#cite_note-Dieter_Nohlen_p._430-74) However, the economy faltered in this period due to a combination of internal and external factors (such as the oil shocks).[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27) The rivalry between the JLP and PNP became intense, and [political and gang-related violence](/wiki/Jamaican_political_conflict "Jamaican political conflict") grew significantly in this period.[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27)

By 1980, Jamaica's gross national product had declined to some 25% below its 1972 level.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Seeking change, in 1980 Jamaicans voted the JLP [back in](/wiki/1980_Jamaican_general_election "1980 Jamaican general election") under [Edward Seaga](/wiki/Edward_Seaga "Edward Seaga"), the JLP winning 51 seats to the PNP's nine seats.[[70]](#cite_note-Dieter_Nohlen_2005_p._430-72)[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27) Firmly anti-Communist, Seaga cut ties with Cuba and sent troops to support the [US invasion of Grenada](/wiki/US_invasion_of_Grenada "US invasion of Grenada") in 1983.[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27) The economic deterioration, however, continued into the mid-1980s, exacerbated by a number of factors. The largest and third-largest alumina producers, [Alpart](/wiki/Alpart "Alpart") and [Alcoa](/wiki/Alcoa "Alcoa"), closed; and there was a significant reduction in production by the second-largest producer, [Alcan](/wiki/Alcan "Alcan").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Reynolds Jamaica Mines, Ltd. left the Jamaican industry. There was also a decline in tourism, which was important to the economy.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Owing to rising foreign and local debt, accompanied by large fiscal deficits, the government sought [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund") (IMF) financing, which was dependent on implementing various austerity measures.[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27) These resulted in strikes in 1985 and a decline in support for the Seaga government, exacerbated by criticism of the government's response to the devastation caused by [Hurricane Gilbert](/wiki/Hurricane_Gilbert "Hurricane Gilbert") in 1988.[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27)[[73]](#cite_note-75) Having now de-emphasised socialism and adopting a more centrist position, Michael Manley and the PNP were [re-elected in 1989](/wiki/1989_Jamaican_general_election "1989 Jamaican general election"), winning 45 seats to the JLP's 15.[[74]](#cite_note-Nohlen_2005_p._430-76)[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27)

The PNP went on to win a string of elections, under Prime Ministers Michael Manley (1989–1992), [P. J. Patterson](/wiki/P._J._Patterson "P. J. Patterson") (1992–2005) and [Portia Simpson-Miller](/wiki/Portia_Simpson-Miller "Portia Simpson-Miller") (2005–2007). In the [1993 Jamaican general election](/wiki/1993_Jamaican_general_election "1993 Jamaican general election"), Patterson led the PNP to victory, winning 52 seats to the JLP's eight seats. Patterson also won the [1997 Jamaican general election](/wiki/1997_Jamaican_general_election "1997 Jamaican general election"), by another landslide margin of 50 seats to the JLP's 10 seats.[[72]](#cite_note-Dieter_Nohlen_p._430-74) Patterson's third consecutive victory came in the [2002 Jamaican general election](/wiki/2002_Jamaican_general_election "2002 Jamaican general election"), when the PNP retained power, but with a reduced seat majority of 34 seats to 26. Patterson stepped down on 26 February 2006, and was replaced by [Portia Simpson-Miller](/wiki/Portia_Simpson-Miller "Portia Simpson-Miller"), Jamaica's first female Prime Minister. The turnout slowly declined during this period of time, from 67.4% in 1993 to 59.1% in 2002.[[74]](#cite_note-Nohlen_2005_p._430-76)

During this period various economic reforms were introduced, such as deregulating the finance sector and floating the Jamaican dollar, as well as greater investment in infrastructure, whilst also retaining a strong social safety net.[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27) Political violence, so prevalent in the previous two decades, declined significantly.[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27)[[75]](#cite_note-77)

In [2007](/wiki/2007_Jamaican_general_election "2007 Jamaican general election") the PNP was defeated by the JLP by a narrow margin of 32 seats to 28, with a turnout of 61.46%.[[76]](#cite_note-78) This election ended 18 years of PNP rule, and [Bruce Golding](/wiki/Bruce_Golding "Bruce Golding") became the new prime minister.[[77]](#cite_note-79) Golding's tenure (2007–2010) was dominated by the effects of the [global recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession"), as well as the fallout from [an attempt](/wiki/2010_Kingston_unrest "2010 Kingston unrest") by Jamaican police and military to arrest drug lord [Christopher Coke](/wiki/Christopher_Coke "Christopher Coke") in 2010 which erupted in violence, resulting in over 70 deaths.[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27)[[78]](#cite_note-OAS_body_raises_concerns_over_Jamaica_as_death_toll_rises-80) As a result of this incident Golding resigned and was replaced by Andrew Holness in 2011.

Independence, however widely celebrated in Jamaica, has been questioned in the early 21st century. In 2011, a survey showed that approximately 60% of Jamaicans believe that the country would have been better off had it remained a British colony, with only 17% believing it would have been worse off, citing as problems years of social and fiscal mismanagement in the country.[[79]](#cite_note-81)[[80]](#cite_note-82) Holness and the JLP were defeated in the [2011 Jamaican general election](/wiki/2011_Jamaican_general_election "2011 Jamaican general election"), which saw Portia Simpson-Miller and the PNP return to power. The number of seats had been increased to 63, and the PNP swept to power with a landslide 42 seats to the JLP's 21. The voter turnout was 53.17%.[[81]](#cite_note-83)

Holness's JLP won the [2016](/wiki/2016_Jamaican_general_election "2016 Jamaican general election") general election narrowly, defeating Simpson-Miller's PNP, on 25 February. The PNP won 31 seats to the JLP's 32. As a result, Simpson-Miller became Opposition Leader for a second time. The voter turnout dipped below 50% for the first time, registering just 48.37%.[[82]](#cite_note-84)

In the [2020 general election](/wiki/2020_Jamaican_general_election "2020 Jamaican general election"), Andrew Holness secured a second consecutive win for the Jamaica Labour Party, winning 49 seats to 14 won by the PNP, led this time by [Peter Phillips](/wiki/Peter_Phillips_(Jamaican_politician) "Peter Phillips (Jamaican politician)"). The last time a consecutive win occurred for the JLP was in 1980. However, the turnout at this election was just 37%, probably affected by the coronavirus pandemic. However, this low turnout was a trend that continued in subsequent elections.[[83]](#cite_note-85)

In the [2025 Jamaican general election](/wiki/2025_Jamaican_general_election "2025 Jamaican general election"), Holness created history, securing a third consecutive term for the JLP, the first time the party had won three general elections in a row. However, this victory was achieved with a much-reduced majority. The JLP won 34 seats to the 29 won by the [Mark Golding](/wiki/Mark_Golding "Mark Golding") led PNP. The turnout was again low, at just 39.5%.[[84]](#cite_note-86)

## Government and politics

Main article: [Politics of Jamaica](/wiki/Politics_of_Jamaica "Politics of Jamaica")

Further information: [Foreign relations of Jamaica](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Jamaica "Foreign relations of Jamaica") and [Republicanism in Jamaica](/wiki/Republicanism_in_Jamaica "Republicanism in Jamaica")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/King_Charles_III_%28July_2023%29.jpg/120px-King_Charles_III_%28July_2023%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_Charles_III_(July_2023).jpg)

[King Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III")  
[Monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Jamaica "Monarchy of Jamaica")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Patrick_Allen_Coronation_2023.jpg/120px-Patrick_Allen_Coronation_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Patrick_Allen_Coronation_2023.jpg)

[Patrick Allen](/wiki/Patrick_Allen_(governor-general) "Patrick Allen (governor-general)")  
[Governor-General](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Jamaica "Governor-General of Jamaica")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Prime_Minister_Narendra_Modi_meets_the_Prime_Minister_of_Jamaica%2C_Dr._Andrew_Holness_at_Hyderabad_House%2C_in_New_Delhi_on_October_01%2C_2024_%28cropped%29.jpg/120px-Prime_Minister_Narendra_Modi_meets_the_Prime_Minister_of_Jamaica%2C_Dr._Andrew_Holness_at_Hyderabad_House%2C_in_New_Delhi_on_October_01%2C_2024_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prime_Minister_Narendra_Modi_meets_the_Prime_Minister_of_Jamaica,_Dr._Andrew_Holness_at_Hyderabad_House,_in_New_Delhi_on_October_01,_2024_(cropped).jpg)

[Andrew Holness](/wiki/Andrew_Holness "Andrew Holness")  
[Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Jamaica "Prime Minister of Jamaica")

Jamaica is a [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") and [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy").[[8]](#cite_note-CIA_World_Factbook_–_Jamaica-9)[[85]](#cite_note-EBJ-87) The head of state is the [King of Jamaica](/wiki/King_of_Jamaica "King of Jamaica") (currently [King Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III")),[[86]](#cite_note-88) represented locally by the [Governor-General of Jamaica](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Jamaica "Governor-General of Jamaica").[[87]](#cite_note-89)[[8]](#cite_note-CIA_World_Factbook_–_Jamaica-9)[[85]](#cite_note-EBJ-87) The governor-general is nominated by the Prime Minister of Jamaica and the entire Cabinet and then formally appointed by the monarch. All the members of the Cabinet are appointed by the governor-general on the advice of the prime minister. The monarch and the governor-general serve largely ceremonial roles, apart from their [reserve powers](/wiki/Reserve_power "Reserve power") for use in certain constitutional crisis situations. The position of the monarch has been a matter of [continuing debate](/wiki/Republicanism_in_Jamaica "Republicanism in Jamaica") in Jamaica for many years; currently both major political parties are committed to transitioning to a republic with a president.[[88]](#cite_note-90)[[89]](#cite_note-91)

Jamaica's current [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Jamaica "Constitution of Jamaica") was drafted in 1962 by a bipartisan joint committee of the Jamaican legislature. It came into force with the Jamaica Independence Act, 1962, which was passed by the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"), which gave Jamaica independence.[[85]](#cite_note-EBJ-87)

The [Parliament of Jamaica](/wiki/Parliament_of_Jamaica "Parliament of Jamaica") is [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism"), consisting of the House of Representatives (Lower House) and the Senate (Upper House). Members of the House (known as Members of Parliament or *MPs*) are directly elected, and the member of the House of Representatives who, in the governor-general's best judgement, is best able to command the confidence of a majority of the members of that House, is appointed by the governor-general to be the prime minister. Senators are nominated jointly by the prime minister and the parliamentary [Leader of the Opposition](/wiki/Opposition_(parliamentary) "Opposition (parliamentary)") and are then appointed by the governor-general.[[85]](#cite_note-EBJ-87)

The [Judiciary of Jamaica](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Jamaica "Judiciary of Jamaica") operates on a [common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law") system derived from [English law](/wiki/English_law "English law") and [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") precedents.[[85]](#cite_note-EBJ-87) The court of final appeal is the [Judicial Committee of the Privy Council](/wiki/Judicial_Committee_of_the_Privy_Council "Judicial Committee of the Privy Council"), a court of the [British Crown](/wiki/British_Crown "British Crown") based in [London](/wiki/London "London"). A proposal has been made to replace the Privy Council with the [Caribbean Court of Justice](/wiki/Caribbean_Court_of_Justice "Caribbean Court of Justice") as part of a wider push toward full [decolonization](/wiki/Decolonization "Decolonization").[[90]](#cite_note-92)

### Political parties and elections

Jamaica has traditionally had a [two-party system](/wiki/Two-party_system "Two-party system"), with power often alternating between the [People's National Party](/wiki/People%27s_National_Party "People's National Party") (PNP) and [Jamaica Labour Party](/wiki/Jamaica_Labour_Party "Jamaica Labour Party") (JLP).[[85]](#cite_note-EBJ-87) The party with current administrative and legislative power is the Jamaica Labour Party, after its 2020 victory. There are also several minor parties who have yet to gain a seat in parliament; the largest of these is the [National Democratic Movement](/wiki/National_Democratic_Movement_(Jamaica) "National Democratic Movement (Jamaica)") (NDM).

### Military

Main article: [Jamaica Defence Force](/wiki/Jamaica_Defence_Force "Jamaica Defence Force")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/FN_FAL_DA-SD-04-01067.jpg/250px-FN_FAL_DA-SD-04-01067.jpg)](/wiki/File:FN_FAL_DA-SD-04-01067.jpg)

Jamaican soldiers training to fire the [L1A1 Self-Loading Rifle](/wiki/L1A1_Self-Loading_Rifle "L1A1 Self-Loading Rifle") in 2002

The Jamaica Defence Force (JDF) is the small but professional military force of Jamaica.[[8]](#cite_note-CIA_World_Factbook_–_Jamaica-9) The JDF is based on the British military model with similar organisation, training, weapons and traditions. Once chosen, officer candidates are sent to one of several British or Canadian basic officer courses depending on the arm of service. Enlisted soldiers are given basic training at Up Park Camp or JDF Training Depot, Newcastle, both in St. Andrew. As with the British model, NCOs are given several levels of professional training as they rise up the ranks. Additional military schools are available for speciality training in Canada, the United States and the United Kingdom.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The JDF is directly descended from the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army")'s [West India Regiment](/wiki/West_India_Regiment "West India Regiment"), which was formed during the colonial era.[[91]](#cite_note-jdfhistory-93) The West India Regiment was used extensively throughout the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") in policing the empire from 1795 to 1926. Other units in the JDF heritage include the early colonial Jamaica Militia, the Kingston Infantry Volunteers of WWI and reorganised into the Jamaican Infantry Volunteers in World War II. The West Indies Regiment was reformed in 1958 as part of the [West Indies Federation](/wiki/West_Indies_Federation "West Indies Federation"), after dissolution of the Federation the JDF was established.[[92]](#cite_note-94)

The Jamaica Defence Force (JDF) comprises an [infantry Regiment](/wiki/The_Jamaica_Regiment "The Jamaica Regiment") and Reserve Corps, an Air Wing, a Coast Guard fleet and a supporting Engineering Unit.[[93]](#cite_note-JDFstructure-95) The infantry regiment contains the 1st, 2nd and 3rd (National Reserve) battalions. The JDF Air Wing is divided into three flight units, a training unit, a support unit and the JDF Air Wing (National Reserve). The Coast Guard is divided between seagoing crews and support crews who conduct maritime safety and maritime law enforcement as well as defence-related operations.[[94]](#cite_note-cgroles-96)

The role of the support battalion is to provide support to boost numbers in combat and issue competency training in order to allow for the readiness of the force.[[95]](#cite_note-csbattalion-97) The 1st Engineer Regiment was formed due to an increased demand for military engineers and their role is to provide engineering services whenever and wherever they are needed.[[96]](#cite_note-engineerhistory-98) The Headquarters JDF contains the JDF Commander, Command Staff as well as Intelligence, Judge Advocate office, Administrative and Procurement sections.[[97]](#cite_note-jdfhq-99)

In recent years the JDF has been called on to assist the nation's police, the [Jamaica Constabulary Force](/wiki/Jamaica_Constabulary_Force "Jamaica Constabulary Force") (JCF), in fighting drug smuggling and a rising crime rate which includes one of the highest murder rates in the world. JDF units actively conduct armed patrols with the JCF in high-crime areas and known gang neighbourhoods. There has been vocal controversy as well as support of this JDF role. In early 2005, an Opposition leader, Edward Seaga, called for the merger of the JDF and JCF. This has not garnered support in either organisation nor among the majority of citizens.[[98]](#cite_note-100) In 2017, Jamaica signed the UN [treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Prohibition_of_Nuclear_Weapons "Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons").[[99]](#cite_note-101) In September 2024 the AP reported that "20 soldiers and four police officers are scheduled to arrive in Haiti to support the UN-Kenya lead mission and they would provide command, planning and logistics support." Jamaica's PM had pledged a total of 170 soldiers and 30 police officers, but said that all would not deploy at once.[[100]](#cite_note-102)

### Administrative divisions

Main article: [Parishes of Jamaica](/wiki/Parishes_of_Jamaica "Parishes of Jamaica")

Jamaica is divided into 14 [parishes](/wiki/Parishes_of_Jamaica "Parishes of Jamaica"), which are grouped into three historic [counties](/wiki/Counties_of_Jamaica "Counties of Jamaica") that have no administrative relevance.[[85]](#cite_note-EBJ-87)

In the context of [local government](/wiki/Local_government "Local government") the parishes are designated "Local Authorities". These local authorities are further styled as "Municipal Corporations", which are either city municipalities or town municipalities.[[101]](#cite_note-localgovernemtn-103) Any new city municipality must have a population of at least 50,000, and a town municipality a number set by the Minister of Local Government.[[101]](#cite_note-localgovernemtn-103) There are currently no town municipalities.

The local governments of the parishes of Kingston and St. Andrews are consolidated as the city municipality of Kingston & St. Andrew Municipal Corporation. The newest city municipality is the Municipality of Portmore, created 2003. While it is geographically located within the parish of St. Catherine, it is governed independently.

## Geography and environment

Main articles: [Geography of Jamaica](/wiki/Geography_of_Jamaica "Geography of Jamaica") and [Geology of Jamaica](/wiki/Geology_of_Jamaica "Geology of Jamaica")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Doctors-Cave-Beach.jpg/250px-Doctors-Cave-Beach.jpg)](/wiki/File:Doctors-Cave-Beach.jpg)

[Doctor's Cave Beach Club](/wiki/Doctor%27s_Cave_Beach_Club "Doctor's Cave Beach Club") is a popular destination in [Montego Bay](/wiki/Montego_Bay "Montego Bay").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Start_of_the_Blue_Mountains_just_north_of_Kingston%2C_Jamaica.jpg/250px-Start_of_the_Blue_Mountains_just_north_of_Kingston%2C_Jamaica.jpg)](/wiki/File:Start_of_the_Blue_Mountains_just_north_of_Kingston,_Jamaica.jpg)

[Blue Mountains](/wiki/Blue_Mountains_(Jamaica) "Blue Mountains (Jamaica)")

Jamaica is the third-largest island in the Caribbean.[[102]](#cite_note-jamaica_background-104) It lies between latitudes [17°](/wiki/17th_parallel_north "17th parallel north") and [19°N](/wiki/19th_parallel_north "19th parallel north"), and longitudes [76°](/wiki/76th_meridian_west "76th meridian west") and [79°W](/wiki/79th_meridian_west "79th meridian west"). Mountains dominate the interior: the Don Figuerero, Santa Cruz, and May Day mountains in the west, the [Dry Harbour Mountains](/wiki/Dry_Harbour_Mountains "Dry Harbour Mountains") in the centre, and the [John Crow Mountains](/wiki/John_Crow_Mountains "John Crow Mountains") and [Blue Mountains](/wiki/Blue_Mountains_(Jamaica) "Blue Mountains (Jamaica)") in the east, the latter containing [Blue Mountain Peak](/wiki/Blue_Mountain_Peak "Blue Mountain Peak"), Jamaica's tallest mountain at 2,256 m.[[8]](#cite_note-CIA_World_Factbook_–_Jamaica-9)[[85]](#cite_note-EBJ-87) They are surrounded by a narrow coastal plain.[[103]](#cite_note-Geogofjam-105)[[8]](#cite_note-CIA_World_Factbook_–_Jamaica-9) Jamaica has two cities, the first being [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_Jamaica "Kingston, Jamaica"), the capital city and centre of business, located on the south coast and the second being [Montego Bay](/wiki/Montego_Bay "Montego Bay"), one of the best known cities in the Caribbean for tourism, located on the north coast. [Kingston Harbour](/wiki/Kingston_Harbour "Kingston Harbour") is the seventh-largest natural harbour in the world,[[104]](#cite_note-portauthority-106) which contributed to the city being designated as the capital in 1872. Other towns of note include [Portmore](/wiki/Portmore,_Jamaica "Portmore, Jamaica"), [Spanish Town](/wiki/Spanish_Town "Spanish Town"), [Savanna la Mar](/wiki/Savanna_la_Mar "Savanna la Mar"), [Mandeville](/wiki/Mandeville,_Jamaica "Mandeville, Jamaica") and the resort towns of [Ocho Ríos](/wiki/Ocho_R%C3%ADos "Ocho Ríos"), [Port Antonio](/wiki/Port_Antonio "Port Antonio") and [Negril](/wiki/Negril "Negril").[[105]](#cite_note-majortownsandcities-107)

Tourist attractions include [Dunn's River Falls](/wiki/Dunn%27s_River_Falls "Dunn's River Falls") in St. Ann, YS Falls in St. Elizabeth, the Blue Lagoon in [Portland](/wiki/Portland_Parish "Portland Parish"), a dormant volcano's crater,[[106]](#cite_note-108) and [Port Royal](/wiki/Port_Royal "Port Royal"), site of a major earthquake in 1692 that helped form the island's [Palisadoes](/wiki/Palisadoes "Palisadoes") tombolo.[[107]](#cite_note-kingston-109)

Among the variety of terrestrial, aquatic and marine ecosystems are dry and wet limestone forests, rainforest, riparian woodland, wetlands, caves, rivers, seagrass beds and coral reefs. The authorities have recognised the tremendous significance and potential of the environment and have designated some of the more "fertile" areas as "protected". Among the island's protected areas are the [Cockpit Country](/wiki/Cockpit_Country "Cockpit Country"), [Hellshire Hills](/wiki/Jamaican_dry_forests "Jamaican dry forests"), and Litchfield forest reserves. In 1992, Jamaica's first marine park, covering nearly 15 square kilometres (5.8 sq mi), was established in [Montego Bay](/wiki/Montego_Bay "Montego Bay"). [Portland Bight Protected Area](/wiki/Portland_Bight_Protected_Area "Portland Bight Protected Area") was designated in 1999.[[108]](#cite_note-110) The following year [Blue and John Crow Mountains National Park](/wiki/Blue_and_John_Crow_Mountains_National_Park "Blue and John Crow Mountains National Park") was created, covering roughly 300 square miles (780 km2) of a wilderness area which supports thousands of tree and fern species and rare animals.

There are several small islands off Jamaica's coast, most notably those in [Portland Bight](/wiki/Portland_Bight "Portland Bight") such as Pigeon Island, Salt Island, Dolphin Island, Long Island, [Great Goat Island](/wiki/Great_Goat_Island "Great Goat Island") and [Little Goat Island](/wiki/Little_Goat_Island "Little Goat Island"), and also [Lime Cay](/wiki/Lime_Cay "Lime Cay") located further east. Much further out – some 50–80 km off the south coast – lie the very small [Morant Cays](/wiki/Morant_Cays "Morant Cays") and [Pedro Cays](/wiki/Pedro_Cays "Pedro Cays").

### Climate

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Koppen-Geiger_Map_JAM_present.svg/250px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_JAM_present.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_JAM_present.svg)

Köppen climate classification of Jamaica

The climate in Jamaica is tropical, with hot and humid weather, although higher inland regions are more temperate.[[109]](#cite_note-climate-111)[[85]](#cite_note-EBJ-87) Some regions on the south coast, such as the Liguanea Plain and the Pedro Plains, are relatively dry [rain-shadow](/wiki/Rain_shadow "Rain shadow") areas.[[110]](#cite_note-Gleaner_Climate-112)

Jamaica lies within the [Main Development Region](/wiki/Main_Development_Region "Main Development Region") for Atlantic [tropical cyclone](/wiki/Tropical_cyclone "Tropical cyclone") activity, and because of this, the island sometimes suffers significant storm damage.[[111]](#cite_note-jamhurricanes-113)[[85]](#cite_note-EBJ-87) Hurricanes [Charlie](/wiki/Hurricane_Charlie_(1951) "Hurricane Charlie (1951)") and [Gilbert](/wiki/Hurricane_Gilbert "Hurricane Gilbert") hit Jamaica directly in 1951 and 1988, respectively, causing major damage and many deaths. In the 2000s (decade), hurricanes [Ivan](/wiki/Hurricane_Ivan "Hurricane Ivan"), [Dean](/wiki/Hurricane_Dean_(2007) "Hurricane Dean (2007)"), and [Gustav](/wiki/Hurricane_Gustav "Hurricane Gustav") also brought severe weather to the island. In 2025, [Hurricane Melissa](/wiki/Hurricane_Melissa "Hurricane Melissa") made landfall as a Category 5, far surpassing Gilbert as the strongest landfalling storm in Jamaican history.

### Flora and fauna

See also: [Jamaican moist forests](/wiki/Jamaican_moist_forests "Jamaican moist forests") and [Jamaican dry forests](/wiki/Jamaican_dry_forests "Jamaican dry forests")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Red-billed_streamertail%28_Trochilus_polytmus%29_adult_male_2.jpg/250px-Red-billed_streamertail%28_Trochilus_polytmus%29_adult_male_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Red-billed_streamertail(_Trochilus_polytmus)_adult_male_2.jpg)

Jamaica's national bird, a [red-billed streamertail](/wiki/Red-billed_streamertail "Red-billed streamertail")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Epicrates_subflavus.jpg/250px-Epicrates_subflavus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Epicrates_subflavus.jpg)

[Jamaican boa](/wiki/Jamaican_boa "Jamaican boa")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Stoplight-parrotfish.jpg/250px-Stoplight-parrotfish.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stoplight-parrotfish.jpg)

[Jamaican parrotfish](/wiki/Parrotfish "Parrotfish")

Jamaica's climate is tropical, supporting diverse ecosystems with a wealth of plants and animals. Its plant life has changed considerably over the centuries; when the Spanish arrived in 1494, except for small agricultural clearings, the country was deeply forested. The European settlers cut down the great timber trees for building and ships' supplies, and cleared the plains, savannas, and mountain slopes for intense agricultural cultivation.[[85]](#cite_note-EBJ-87) Many new plants were introduced including sugarcane, bananas, and citrus trees.[[85]](#cite_note-EBJ-87)

Jamaica is home to about 3,000 species of [native flowering plants](/wiki/Flowering_plant "Flowering plant") (of which over 1,000 are [endemic](/wiki/Endemic "Endemic") and 200 are species of [orchid](/wiki/Orchidaceae "Orchidaceae")), thousands of species of non-flowering flora, and about 20 [botanical gardens](/wiki/Botanical_garden "Botanical garden"), some of which are several hundred years old.[[112]](#cite_note-114)[[113]](#cite_note-115) Areas of heavy rainfall also contain stands of bamboo, ferns, ebony, mahogany, and rosewood. Cactus and similar dry-area plants are found along the south and southwest coastal area. Parts of the west and southwest consist of large grasslands, with scattered stands of trees. Jamaica is home to three terrestrial [ecoregions](/wiki/Ecoregion "Ecoregion"), the [Jamaican moist forests](/wiki/Jamaican_moist_forests "Jamaican moist forests"), [Jamaican dry forests](/wiki/Jamaican_dry_forests "Jamaican dry forests"), and [Greater Antilles mangroves](/wiki/Greater_Antilles_mangroves "Greater Antilles mangroves").

Jamaica's fauna, typical of the Caribbean, includes highly diversified wildlife with many endemic species. As with other oceanic islands, land mammals are mostly several species of [bats](/wiki/Bat "Bat") of which at least three endemic species are found only in Cockpit Country, one of which is at-risk. Other species of bat include the [fig-eating](/wiki/Jamaican_fig-eating_bat "Jamaican fig-eating bat") and [hairy-tailed bats](/wiki/Lasiurus "Lasiurus"). The only non-bat native mammal extant in Jamaica is the [Jamaican hutia](/wiki/Jamaican_hutia "Jamaican hutia"), locally known as the coney.[[85]](#cite_note-EBJ-87) Introduced mammals such as [wild boar](/wiki/Wild_boar "Wild boar") and the [small Asian mongoose](/wiki/Small_Asian_mongoose "Small Asian mongoose") are also common. Jamaica is also home to about 50 species of reptiles,[[114]](#cite_note-116) the largest of which is the [American crocodile](/wiki/American_crocodile "American crocodile"); however, it is only present within the Black River and a few other areas. Lizards such as [anoles](/wiki/Anole "Anole"), [iguanas](/wiki/Iguana "Iguana") and snakes such as racers and the [Jamaican boa](/wiki/Jamaican_boa "Jamaican boa") (the largest snake on the island), are common in areas such as the Cockpit Country. None of Jamaica's eight species of native snakes is venomous.[[115]](#cite_note-117)

Jamaica is home to about 289 species of birds of which 27 are endemic including the endangered [black-Billed parrots](/wiki/Black-billed_amazon "Black-billed amazon") and the [Jamaican blackbird](/wiki/Jamaican_blackbird "Jamaican blackbird"), both of which are only found in Cockpit Country. It is also the indigenous home to four species of [hummingbirds](/wiki/Hummingbird "Hummingbird") (three of which are found nowhere else in the world): the [black-billed streamertail](/wiki/Black-billed_streamertail "Black-billed streamertail"), the [Jamaican mango](/wiki/Jamaican_mango "Jamaican mango"), the [Vervain hummingbird](/wiki/Vervain_hummingbird "Vervain hummingbird"), and [red-billed streamertails](/wiki/Red-billed_streamertail "Red-billed streamertail"). The red-billed streamertail, known locally as the "doctor bird", is Jamaica's National Symbol.[[116]](#cite_note-118)[[85]](#cite_note-EBJ-87) Other notable species include the [Jamaican tody](/wiki/Jamaican_tody "Jamaican tody") and the [Greater flamingo](/wiki/Greater_flamingo "Greater flamingo"),[[117]](#cite_note-119)

One species of freshwater turtle is native to Jamaica, the [Jamaican slider](/wiki/Jamaican_slider "Jamaican slider"). It is found only on Jamaica and on a few islands in [the Bahamas](/wiki/The_Bahamas "The Bahamas"). In addition, many types of frogs are common on the island, especially [treefrogs](/wiki/Treefrog "Treefrog").

Jamaican waters contain considerable resources of fresh and saltwater fish.[[118]](#cite_note-120) The chief varieties of saltwater fish are [kingfish](/wiki/King_mackerel "King mackerel"), [jack](/wiki/Carangidae "Carangidae"), [mackerel](/wiki/Mackerel "Mackerel"), [whiting](/wiki/Whiting_(fish) "Whiting (fish)"), [bonito](/wiki/Bonito "Bonito"), and [tuna](/wiki/Tuna "Tuna"). Fish that occasionally enter freshwater and estuarine environments include [snook](/wiki/Centropomidae "Centropomidae"), [jewfish](/wiki/Epinephelus_itajara "Epinephelus itajara"), [mangrove snapper](/wiki/Mangrove_snapper "Mangrove snapper"), and [mullets](/wiki/Mullet_(fish) "Mullet (fish)"). Fish that spend the majority of their lives in Jamaica's fresh waters include many species of [livebearers](/wiki/Livebearer "Livebearer"), [killifish](/wiki/Killifish "Killifish"), freshwater [gobies](/wiki/Goby "Goby"), the mountain mullet, and the [American eel](/wiki/American_eel "American eel"). [Tilapia](/wiki/Tilapia "Tilapia") have been introduced from Africa for aquaculture, and are very common. Also visible in the waters surrounding Jamaica are dolphins, [parrotfish](/wiki/Parrotfish "Parrotfish"), and the endangered [manatee](/wiki/West_Indian_manatee "West Indian manatee").[[119]](#cite_note-121)

Insects and other invertebrates are abundant, including the world's largest centipede, the [Amazonian giant centipede](/wiki/Amazonian_giant_centipede "Amazonian giant centipede"). Jamaica is the home to about 150 species of butterflies and moths, including 35 indigenous species and 22 subspecies. It is also the native home to the [Jamaican swallowtail](/wiki/Homerus_swallowtail "Homerus swallowtail"), the western hemisphere's largest butterfly.[[120]](#cite_note-122)

### Aquatic life

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/RaftShilouette20221019_134124.jpg/250px-RaftShilouette20221019_134124.jpg)](/wiki/File:RaftShilouette20221019_134124.jpg)

White River Rafting

Coral reef ecosystems are important because they provide people with a source of livelihood, food, recreation, and medicinal compounds and protect the land on which they live.[[121]](#cite_note-:03-123) Jamaica relies on the ocean and its ecosystem for its development. However, the marine life in Jamaica is also being affected. There could be many factors that contribute to marine life not having the best health. Jamaica's geological origin, topographical features and seasonal high rainfall make it susceptible to a range of natural hazards that can affect the coastal and oceanic environments. These include storm surge, slope failures (landslides), earthquakes, floods and hurricanes.[[122]](#cite_note-:22-124) Coral reefs in the Negril Marine Park (NMP), Jamaica, have been increasingly impacted by [nutrient pollution](/wiki/Nutrient_pollution "Nutrient pollution") and macroalgal blooms following decades of intensive development as a major tourist destination.[[123]](#cite_note-125)

Another one of those factors could include tourism: being that Jamaica is a very touristy place, the island draws numerous people travelling here from all over the world. The Jamaican tourism industry accounts for 32% of total employment and 36% of the country's GDP and is largely based on the sun, sea and sand, the last two of these attributes being dependent on healthy coral reef ecosystems.[[121]](#cite_note-:03-123) Because of Jamaica's tourism, they have developed a study to see if the tourist would be willing to help financially to manage their marine ecosystem because Jamaica alone is unable to. The ocean connects all the countries all over the world, however, everyone and everything is affecting the flow and life in the ocean. Jamaica is a very touristy place specifically because of their beaches. If their oceans are not functioning at their best then the well-being of Jamaica and the people who live there will start to deteriorate. According to the OECD, oceans contribute $1.5 trillion annually in value-added to the overall economy.[[124]](#cite_note-:12-126) A developing country on an island will get the majority of their revenue from their ocean.

### Pollution

Pollution comes from run-off, sewage systems, and garbage. However, this typically all ends up in the ocean after there is rain or floods. Everything that ends up in the water changes the quality and balance of the ocean. Poor coastal water quality has adversely affected fisheries, tourism and mariculture, as well as undermining biological sustainability of the living resources of ocean and coastal habitats.[[122]](#cite_note-:22-124) Jamaica imports and exports many goods through their waters. Some of the imports that go into Jamaica include petroleum and petroleum products. Issues include accidents at sea; risk of spills through local and international transport of petroleum and petroleum products.[[122]](#cite_note-:22-124) Oil spills can disrupt the marine life with chemicals that are not normally found in the ocean. Other forms of pollution also occur in Jamaica. Solid waste disposal mechanisms in Jamaica are currently inadequate.[[122]](#cite_note-:22-124) The solid waste gets into the water through rainfall forces. Solid waste is also harmful to wildlife, particularly birds, fish and turtles that feed at the surface of the water and mistake floating debris for food.[[122]](#cite_note-:22-124) For example, plastic can be caught around birds' and turtles' necks, making it difficult to eat and breath as they begin to grow, causing the plastic to get tighter around their necks. Pieces of plastic, metal, and glass can be mistaken for the food fish eat. Each Jamaican generates 1 kg (2 lbs) of waste per day; only 70% of this is collected by National Solid Waste Management Authority (NSWMA) — the remaining 30% is either burnt or disposed of in gullies/waterways.[[125]](#cite_note-127)

### Environmental policies

There are policies that are being put into place to help preserve the ocean and the life below water. The goal of integrated coastal zone management (ICZM) is to improve the quality of life of human communities who depend on coastal resources while maintaining the biological diversity and productivity of coastal ecosystems.[[122]](#cite_note-:22-124) Developing an underdeveloped country can impact the oceans ecosystem because of all the construction that would be done to develop the country. Over-building, driven by powerful market forces as well as poverty among some sectors of the population, and destructive exploitation contribute to the decline of ocean and coastal resources.[[122]](#cite_note-:22-124) Developing practices that will contribute to the lives of the people but also to the life of the ocean and its ecosystem. Some of these practices include: Develop sustainable fisheries practices, ensure sustainable mariculture techniques and practices, sustainable management of shipping, and promote [sustainable tourism](/wiki/Sustainable_tourism "Sustainable tourism") practices.[[122]](#cite_note-:22-124)

## Demographics

Further information: [Demographics of Jamaica](/wiki/Demographics_of_Jamaica "Demographics of Jamaica") and [Jamaican people](/wiki/Jamaican_people "Jamaican people")

### Ethnic origins

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Street_in_Montigo_Bay_Jamaica_Photo_D_Ramey_Logan.jpg/250px-Street_in_Montigo_Bay_Jamaica_Photo_D_Ramey_Logan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Street_in_Montigo_Bay_Jamaica_Photo_D_Ramey_Logan.jpg)

[Montego Bay](/wiki/Montego_Bay "Montego Bay"), Jamaica's second-largest city
