Jamaica's diverse ethnic roots are reflected in the national motto ["Out of Many One People"](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Jamaica "Coat of arms of Jamaica"). Some dispute the appropriateness of the motto because Jamaicans are overwhelmingly of a single race. The Jamaican founding fathers were mostly White or [brown](/wiki/Brown_(racial_classification) "Brown (racial classification)") men and unrepresentative of the views of the country's majority Black population.[[126]](#cite_note-128)[[127]](#cite_note-129)[[128]](#cite_note-130)

Most of the population of 2,812,000 (July 2018 est.)[[8]](#cite_note-CIA_World_Factbook_–_Jamaica-9) are of African or partially African descent, with many being able to trace their origins to the West African countries of present-day [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana "Ghana") and [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria").[[85]](#cite_note-EBJ-87)[[129]](#cite_note-131) Other major ancestral areas are [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"),[[60]](#cite_note-tortello-62) [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"), and [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] It is uncommon for Jamaicans to identify themselves by race as is prominent in other countries such as the United States, with most Jamaicans seeing Jamaican nationality as an identity in and of itself, identifying as simply being "Jamaican" regardless of ethnicity.[[130]](#cite_note-132)[[131]](#cite_note-jamaicans.com-133) A study found that the average admixture on the island was 78.3% Sub-Saharan African, 16.0% [European](/wiki/White_Jamaican "White Jamaican"), and 5.7% [East Asian](/wiki/East_Asians "East Asians").[[132]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-134)

A more precise study was conducted by the local University of the West Indies – Jamaica's population is more accurately 76.3% African descent or Black, 15.1% Afro-European (or locally called the Brown Man or Browning Class), 3.4% East Indian and Afro-East Indian, 3.2% White, 1.2% Chinese and 0.8% Other.[[1]](#cite_note-uwi.edu-1) The [Jamaican Maroons](/wiki/Jamaican_Maroons "Jamaican Maroons") of Accompong and other settlements are the descendants of African slaves who fled the plantations for the interior, where they set up their own autonomous communities.[[133]](#cite_note-135)[[134]](#cite_note-136)[[135]](#cite_note-Craton,_Michael_1982,_p._70-137) Many Maroons continue to have their own traditions and speak their own language, known locally as [Kromanti](/wiki/Kromanti "Kromanti").[[136]](#cite_note-138)

Asians form the third-largest group (after the Browning Class – i.e. descendants of the mulattos during slavery and other interracial mixtures subsequent to emancipation) and include Indo-Jamaicans and Chinese Jamaicans.[[137]](#cite_note-cia.gov-139) Most are descended from indentured workers brought by the British colonial government to fill labour shortages following the abolition of slavery in 1838. Along with their Indian counterparts, [Chinese Jamaicans](/wiki/Chinese_Jamaicans "Chinese Jamaicans") have also played an integral part in Jamaica's community and history.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] There are about 20,000 Jamaicans who have [Lebanese](/wiki/Lebanese_Jamaicans "Lebanese Jamaicans") and [Syrian](/wiki/Syrian "Syrian") ancestry.[[138]](#cite_note-140) Most were Christian immigrants who fled the [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") occupation of Lebanon in the early 19th century. Eventually, their descendants became very successful politicians and businessmen.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The first wave of English immigrants arrived on the island in 1655 after conquering the Spanish, and they have historically been the dominant group. The first Irish immigrants came to Jamaica in the 1600s as war prisoners and, later, as indentured laborers. In the 1650s, Oliver Cromwell exiled Scots prisoners of war to Jamaica, and as a result, [Scots](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people") and [Scottish Jamaicans](/wiki/Scottish_Jamaicans "Scottish Jamaicans") have made a significant impact on the island, including many Jamaicans having common Scottish surnames, place names across the island of Scottish origins, the establishments of [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian") churches by Scots across the island in the 17th century, as well as the national flag of Jamaica taking inspiration from the [national flag of Scotland](/wiki/National_flag_of_Scotland "National flag of Scotland").[[47]](#cite_note-adventuresfromelle.com-49)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Northern_suburbs_of_Kingston%2C_Jamaica.jpg/250px-Northern_suburbs_of_Kingston%2C_Jamaica.jpg)](/wiki/File:Northern_suburbs_of_Kingston,_Jamaica.jpg)

Northern suburbs of [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_Jamaica "Kingston, Jamaica"), Jamaica's capital and largest city

There is also a significant Jamaican population of Portuguese descent that is predominantly of [Sephardic Jewish heritage](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Jamaica "History of the Jews in Jamaica").[[139]](#cite_note-141)[[140]](#cite_note-142)[[141]](#cite_note-143)[[142]](#cite_note-144) The first Jews arrived as explorers from Spain in the 15th century after being forced to choose between expulsion or [conversion to Christianity](/wiki/Converso "Converso"). A small number of them became slave owners or pirates.[[143]](#cite_note-145) Judaism eventually became very influential in Jamaica and can be seen today with many Jewish cemeteries around the country. During [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust"), Jamaica became a refuge for Jews fleeing persecution in Europe.[[144]](#cite_note-146)

By 2015 immigration had increased, coming mainly from China, Haiti, Cuba, Colombia, and Latin America; 20,000 Latin Americans lived in Jamaica.[[145]](#cite_note-147) In 2016, Prime Minister Andrew Holness suggested making Spanish Jamaica's second official language.[[146]](#cite_note-148) About 7,000 Americans live in Jamaica.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Languages

Main articles: [Jamaican Patois](/wiki/Jamaican_Patois "Jamaican Patois") and [Jamaican English](/wiki/Jamaican_English "Jamaican English")

Jamaica is regarded as a [bilingual](/wiki/Bilingual "Bilingual") country, with two major languages used by the population.[[147]](#cite_note-149)[[137]](#cite_note-cia.gov-139) The official language is [(Jamaican) English](/wiki/Jamaican_English "Jamaican English"), which is "used in all domains of public life", including the government, the legal system, the media, and education. However, the primary spoken language is an [English-based creole language](/wiki/English-based_creole_languages "English-based creole languages") called [Jamaican Patois](/wiki/Jamaican_Patois "Jamaican Patois") (or Patwa). The two exist in a dialect continuum, with speakers using a different speech register depending on the context and to whom they speak. "Pure" Patois, though sometimes perceived as merely a particularly aberrant dialect of English, is essentially mutually unintelligible with standard English and linguists consider it a distinct language, though most of its vocabulary originally derives from English.[[85]](#cite_note-EBJ-87) A 2007 survey by the Jamaican Language Unit found that 17.1 percent of the population were [monolingual](/wiki/Monolingualism "Monolingualism") in Jamaican Standard English (JSE), 36.5 percent were monolingual in Patois, and 46.4 percent were bilingual, although earlier surveys had pointed to a greater degree of bilingualism (up to 90 percent).[[148]](#cite_note-150) The Jamaican education system had only in about 2015 begun to offer formal instruction in Patois while retaining JSE as the "official language of instruction".[[149]](#cite_note-151)

Additionally, some Jamaicans use one or more of [Jamaican Sign Language](/wiki/Jamaican_Sign_Language "Jamaican Sign Language") (JSL), [American Sign Language](/wiki/American_Sign_Language "American Sign Language") (ASL) or the declining indigenous [Jamaican Country Sign Language](/wiki/Jamaican_Country_Sign_Language "Jamaican Country Sign Language") (Konchri Sain).[[150]](#cite_note-e25jcs-152) Both JSL and ASL are rapidly replacing Konchri Sain for a variety of reasons.[[150]](#cite_note-e25jcs-152)

### Emigration

Main article: [Jamaican diaspora](/wiki/Jamaican_diaspora "Jamaican diaspora")

Many Jamaicans have [emigrated](/wiki/Emigrated "Emigrated") to other countries, especially the United Kingdom, the United States, and Canada. In the case of the United States, about 20,000 Jamaicans per year are granted permanent residence.[[151]](#cite_note-153) There has also been emigration of Jamaicans to other Caribbeans countries such as Cuba,[[152]](#cite_note-154) Puerto Rico, Guyana, and The Bahamas. It was estimated in 2004 that up to 2.5 million Jamaicans and Jamaican descendants lived abroad.[[153]](#cite_note-155)

About 800,000 [Jamaicans live in the United Kingdom](/wiki/British_Jamaican "British Jamaican"), making them by far the country's largest [African-Caribbean group](/wiki/British_African-Caribbean_community "British African-Caribbean community"). Large-scale migration from Jamaica to the UK occurred primarily in the 1950s and 1960s when the country was still under British rule. There are Jamaican communities in most large UK cities.[[154]](#cite_note-IOMMapping-156) Concentrations of [expatriate](/wiki/Expatriate "Expatriate") Jamaicans are quite considerable in numerous cities in the United States, including [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"), the [Miami](/wiki/South_Florida "South Florida") metro area, [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta,_Georgia "Atlanta, Georgia"), [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago,_Illinois "Chicago, Illinois"), [Orlando](/wiki/Orlando,_Florida "Orlando, Florida"), [Tampa](/wiki/Tampa,_Florida "Tampa, Florida"), [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), [Hartford](/wiki/Hartford,_Connecticut "Hartford, Connecticut"), [Providence](/wiki/Providence,_Rhode_Island "Providence, Rhode Island") and [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles,_California "Los Angeles, California").[[155]](#cite_note-157) In Canada, the Jamaican population is centred in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto"),[[156]](#cite_note-Toronto-158) with smaller communities in cities such as [Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton,_Ontario "Hamilton, Ontario"), [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), [Winnipeg](/wiki/Winnipeg "Winnipeg"), [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver") and [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa").[[157]](#cite_note-159) Jamaican Canadians comprise about 30% of the entire [Black Canadian population](/wiki/Black_Canadians "Black Canadians").[[158]](#cite_note-jamaicans-160)[[159]](#cite_note-161)
A notable though much smaller group of emigrants are [Jamaicans in Ethiopia](/wiki/Jamaicans_in_Ethiopia "Jamaicans in Ethiopia"). These are mostly Rastafarians, in whose theological worldview Africa is the promised land, or "Zion", or more specifically Ethiopia, due to reverence in which former Ethiopian Emperor [Haile Selassie](/wiki/Haile_Selassie "Haile Selassie") is held.[[160]](#cite_note-162) Most live in the small town of [Shashamane](/wiki/Shashamane "Shashamane") about 150 miles (240 km) south of the capital [Addis Ababa](/wiki/Addis_Ababa "Addis Ababa").[[161]](#cite_note-163)

## Religion

Main article: [Religion in Jamaica](/wiki/Religion_in_Jamaica "Religion in Jamaica")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Mandeville_Church_by_AAlexander.jpg/250px-Mandeville_Church_by_AAlexander.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mandeville_Church_by_AAlexander.jpg)

Mandeville Church (est. 1816), an [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") church in [Manchester Parish](/wiki/Manchester_Parish "Manchester Parish"). Christianity is the largest religion in Jamaica.

[Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") is the largest religion practised in Jamaica.[[85]](#cite_note-EBJ-87)[[8]](#cite_note-CIA_World_Factbook_–_Jamaica-9) About 70% are [Protestants](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism"); [Catholics](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") are just 2% of the population.[[8]](#cite_note-CIA_World_Factbook_–_Jamaica-9) According to the 2001 census, the country's largest Protestant denominations are the [Church of God](/wiki/Church_of_God_(Anderson,_Indiana) "Church of God (Anderson, Indiana)") (24%), [Seventh-day Adventist Church](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist_Church "Seventh-day Adventist Church") (11%), [Pentecostal](/wiki/Pentecostal "Pentecostal") (10%), [Baptist](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist") (7%), [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") (4%), [United Church](/wiki/United_Church "United Church") (2%), [Methodist](/wiki/Methodist "Methodist") (2%), [Moravian](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church") (1%) and [Plymouth Brethren](/wiki/Plymouth_Brethren "Plymouth Brethren") (1%).[[15]](#cite_note-state2007-16) [Bedwardism](/wiki/Bedwardism "Bedwardism") is a form of Christianity native to the island, sometimes viewed as a separate faith.[[162]](#cite_note-Simpson-164)[[163]](#cite_note-165) The Christian faith gained acceptance as British Christian abolitionists and Baptist missionaries joined educated former slaves in the struggle against slavery.[[164]](#cite_note-166)

The [Rastafari](/wiki/Rastafari "Rastafari") movement has 29,026 adherents, according to the 2011 census, with 25,325 Rastafarian males and 3,701 Rastafarian females.[[15]](#cite_note-state2007-16) The faith originated in Jamaica in the 1930s and though rooted in Christianity it is heavily Afrocentric in its focus, revering figures such as the Jamaican black nationalist [Marcus Garvey](/wiki/Marcus_Garvey "Marcus Garvey") and [Haile Selassie](/wiki/Haile_Selassie "Haile Selassie"), the former Emperor of Ethiopia.[[165]](#cite_note-167)[[85]](#cite_note-EBJ-87) Rastafari has since spread across the globe, especially to areas with large black or African diasporas.[[166]](#cite_note-168)[[167]](#cite_note-169)

Various faiths and traditional religious practices derived from Africa are practised on the island, notably [Kumina](/wiki/Kumina "Kumina"), [Convince](/wiki/Convince "Convince"), [Myal](/wiki/Myal "Myal") and [Obeah](/wiki/Obeah "Obeah").[[168]](#cite_note-170)[[169]](#cite_note-carib-171)[[170]](#cite_note-172)

Other religions in Jamaica include [Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses") (2% population), the [Bahá'í faith](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%27%C3%AD_Faith_in_Jamaica "Bahá'í Faith in Jamaica"), which counts perhaps 8,000 adherents[[171]](#cite_note-173) and 21 [Local Spiritual Assemblies](/wiki/Local_Spiritual_Assembly "Local Spiritual Assembly"),[[172]](#cite_note-4th-174) [Mormonism](/wiki/Mormonism "Mormonism"),[[173]](#cite_note-175) [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), and [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Jamaica "Hinduism in Jamaica").[[174]](#cite_note-176)[[175]](#cite_note-177) The Hindu [Diwali](/wiki/Diwali "Diwali") festival is celebrated yearly among the [Indo-Jamaican](/wiki/Indo-Jamaican "Indo-Jamaican") community.[[176]](#cite_note-178)[[60]](#cite_note-tortello-62)

There is also a small population of about 200 [Jews](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"), who describe themselves as Liberal-Conservative.[[177]](#cite_note-179) The first Jews in Jamaica trace their roots back to early 15th-century Spain and Portugal.[[178]](#cite_note-180) [Kahal Kadosh Shaare Shalom](/wiki/Shaare_Shalom_Synagogue "Shaare Shalom Synagogue"), also known as the United Congregation of Israelites, is a historic synagogue located in the city of [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_Jamaica "Kingston, Jamaica"). Originally built in 1912, it is the official and only Jewish place of worship left on the island. The once abundant Jewish population has voluntarily converted to Christianity over time.[[179]](#cite_note-181) Shaare Shalom is one of the few synagogues in the world that contains sand covered floors and is a popular tourist destination.[[180]](#cite_note-182)[[181]](#cite_note-183)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Hussay_Celebration_in_Jamaica.jpg/250px-Hussay_Celebration_in_Jamaica.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hussay_Celebration_in_Jamaica.jpg)

A historic Ashura celebration in Jamaica, which is known locally as Hussay or Hosay

Other small groups include [Muslims](/wiki/Islam_in_Jamaica "Islam in Jamaica"), who claim 5,000 adherents.[[15]](#cite_note-state2007-16) The Muslim holidays of [Ashura](/wiki/Ashura "Ashura") (known locally as Hussay or [Hosay](/wiki/Hosay "Hosay")) and [Eid](/wiki/Muslim_holidays "Muslim holidays") have been celebrated throughout the island for hundreds of years. In the past, every plantation in each parish celebrated Hosay. Today it has been called an Indian carnival and is perhaps most well known in Clarendon where it is celebrated each August. People of all religions attend the event, showing mutual respect.[[182]](#cite_note-184)[[60]](#cite_note-tortello-62)

## Education

Main article: [Education in Jamaica](/wiki/Education_in_Jamaica "Education in Jamaica")

The emancipation of the slaves heralded the establishment of an education system for the masses. Prior to emancipation there were few schools for educating locals and many sent their children off to England to access quality education.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] After emancipation the West Indian Commission granted a sum of money to establish Elementary Schools, now known as *All Age Schools*. Most of these schools were established by the churches.[[183]](#cite_note-185) This was the genesis of the modern Jamaican school system.

Presently the following categories of schools exist:

* **Early childhood** – Basic, infant and privately operated pre-school. Age cohort: 2 – 5 years.
* **Primary** – Publicly and privately owned (privately owned being called preparatory schools). Ages 3 – 12 years.
* **Secondary** – Publicly and privately owned. Ages 10 – 19 years. The high schools in Jamaica may be either single-sex or co-educational institutions, and many schools follow the traditional [English grammar school model](/wiki/Sixth_form "Sixth form") used throughout the British West Indies.
* **Tertiary** – Community colleges; teachers' colleges, with the Mico Teachers' College (now The MICO University College) being the oldest, founded in 1836; the Shortwood Teachers' College (which was once an all-female teacher training institution); vocational training centres, colleges and universities, publicly and privately owned. There are five local universities: the [University of the West Indies](/wiki/University_of_the_West_Indies "University of the West Indies") (Mona Campus); the [University of Technology, Jamaica](/wiki/University_of_Technology,_Jamaica "University of Technology, Jamaica"), formerly The College of Art Science and Technology (CAST); the [Northern Caribbean University](/wiki/Northern_Caribbean_University "Northern Caribbean University"), formerly West Indies College; the [University of the Commonwealth Caribbean](/wiki/University_of_the_Commonwealth_Caribbean "University of the Commonwealth Caribbean"), formerly the University College of The Caribbean; and the [International University of the Caribbean](/wiki/International_University_of_the_Caribbean "International University of the Caribbean").

Additionally, there are many community and teacher training colleges.

Education is free from the early childhood to secondary levels. There are also opportunities for those who cannot afford further education in the vocational arena, through the Human Employment and Resource Training-National Training Agency (HEART Trust-NTA) programme,[[184]](#cite_note-186) which is opened to all working age national population[[185]](#cite_note-187) and through an extensive scholarship network for the various universities.

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Jamaica](/wiki/Economy_of_Jamaica "Economy of Jamaica")

Jamaica is a [mixed economy](/wiki/Mixed_economy "Mixed economy") with both state enterprises and private sector businesses. Major sectors of the Jamaican economy include [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture"), [mining](/wiki/Mining "Mining"), [manufacturing](/wiki/Manufacturing "Manufacturing"), tourism, [petroleum refining](/wiki/Petroleum_refining_processes "Petroleum refining processes"), [financial](/wiki/Financial "Financial") and [insurance](/wiki/Insurance "Insurance") services.[[85]](#cite_note-EBJ-87) Tourism and mining are the leading earners of [foreign exchange](/wiki/Foreign_exchange_market "Foreign exchange market"). Half the Jamaican economy relies on services, with half of its income coming from services such as tourism. An estimated 4.3 million foreign tourists visit Jamaica every year.[[12]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-13) According to the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank"), Jamaica is an upper-middle income country that, like its Caribbean neighbours, is vulnerable to the [effects of climate change](/wiki/Climate_change_in_the_Caribbean "Climate change in the Caribbean"), flooding, and hurricanes.[[11]](#cite_note-Jamaica_country-12) In 2018, Jamaica represented the [CARICOM Caribbean Community](/wiki/Caribbean_Community "Caribbean Community") at the [G20](/wiki/G20 "G20") and the [G7](/wiki/Group_of_Seven "Group of Seven") annual meetings.[[186]](#cite_note-188) In 2019 Jamaica reported its lowest unemployment rate in 50 years.[[187]](#cite_note-189)

Supported by multilateral financial institutions, Jamaica has, since the early 1980s, sought to implement structural reforms aimed at fostering private sector activity and increasing the role of market forces in resource allocation[[188]](#cite_note-mof.gov.jm-190)[[189]](#cite_note-Jamaica_Observer_newspaper-191)[[190]](#cite_note-Jamaica_Gleaner_newspaper-192) Since 1991, the government has followed a programme of economic liberalisation and stabilisation by removing exchange controls,[[191]](#cite_note-193)[[192]](#cite_note-194) floating the exchange rate,[[193]](#cite_note-195)[[194]](#cite_note-state1-196) cutting [tariffs](/wiki/Tariff "Tariff"),[[195]](#cite_note-197) stabilising the [Jamaican dollar](/wiki/Jamaican_dollar "Jamaican dollar"), reducing [inflation](/wiki/Inflation "Inflation")[[196]](#cite_note-Bloomberg-198) and removing restrictions on [foreign investment](/wiki/Foreign_direct_investment "Foreign direct investment").[[194]](#cite_note-state1-196)[[197]](#cite_note-199) Emphasis has been placed on maintaining strict fiscal discipline, greater openness to trade and financial flows, market liberalisation and reduction in the size of government. During this period, a large share of the economy was returned to private sector ownership through [divestment](/wiki/Divestment "Divestment") and privatisation programmes.[[188]](#cite_note-mof.gov.jm-190)[[189]](#cite_note-Jamaica_Observer_newspaper-191)[[190]](#cite_note-Jamaica_Gleaner_newspaper-192) The free-trade zones at Kingston, Montego Bay and Spanish Town allow duty-free importation, tax-free profits, and free repatriation of export earnings.[[198]](#cite_note-Jamaica_Economy-200)

### Post–independence

Jamaica's economy grew strongly after the years of independence,[[198]](#cite_note-Jamaica_Economy-200) but then stagnated in the 1980s, due to the heavy falls in price of bauxite and fluctuations in the price of agriculture.[[198]](#cite_note-Jamaica_Economy-200)[[85]](#cite_note-EBJ-87) The financial sector was troubled in 1994, with many banks and insurance companies suffering heavy losses and liquidity problems.[[85]](#cite_note-EBJ-87)[[198]](#cite_note-Jamaica_Economy-200) According to the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") Secretariat, "The government set up the Financial Sector Adjustment Company (Finsac) in January 1997 to assist these banks and companies, providing funds in return for equity, and acquired substantial holdings in banks and insurance companies and related companies..." but it only exasperated the problem, and brought the country into large external debt.[[198]](#cite_note-Jamaica_Economy-200) From 2001, once it had restored these banks and companies to financial health, Finsac divested them."[[198]](#cite_note-Jamaica_Economy-200) The [Government of Jamaica](/wiki/Government_of_Jamaica "Government of Jamaica") remains committed to lowering inflation, with a long-term objective of bringing it in line with that of its major trading partners.[[196]](#cite_note-Bloomberg-198)

In 1996 and 1997 there was a decrease in GDP largely due to significant problems in the financial sector and, in 1997, a severe island-wide drought (the worst in 70 years) and hurricane that drastically reduced agricultural production.[[199]](#cite_note-201) In 1997 and 1998, nominal GDP was approximately a high of about 8 percent of GDP and then lowered to 4½ percent of GDP in 1999 and 2000.[[200]](#cite_note-202) The economy in 1997 was marked by low levels of [import](/wiki/Import "Import") growth, high levels of private capital inflows and relative stability in the [foreign exchange market](/wiki/Foreign_exchange_market "Foreign exchange market").[[201]](#cite_note-203)

### Recent advancements

Recent economic performance shows the Jamaican economy is recovering. [Agricultural](/wiki/Agricultural "Agricultural") production, an important engine of growth increased to 5.5% in 2001 compared to the corresponding period in 2000, signalling the first positive growth rate in the sector since January 1997.[[202]](#cite_note-204) In 2018, Jamaica reported a 7.9% increase in [corn](/wiki/Maize "Maize"), 6.1% increase in [plantains](/wiki/Cooking_banana "Cooking banana"), 10.4% increase in [bananas](/wiki/Banana "Banana"), 2.2% increase in [pineapples](/wiki/Pineapple "Pineapple"), 13.3% increase in [dasheen](/wiki/Colocasia_esculenta "Colocasia esculenta"), 24.9% increase in [coconuts](/wiki/Coconut "Coconut"), and a 10.6% increase in whole [milk](/wiki/Milk "Milk") production.[[203]](#cite_note-205) Bauxite and alumina production increased 5.5% from January to December 1998, compared to the corresponding period in 1997. January's bauxite production recorded a 7.1% increase relative to January 1998 and continued expansion of alumina production through 2009 is planned by Alcoa.[[204]](#cite_note-206) Jamaica is the fifth-largest exporter of bauxite in the world, after Australia, China, Brazil and Guinea. The country also exports [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone"), of which it holds large deposits. The government is currently implementing plans to increase its extraction.[[205]](#cite_note-207)

### Tourism industry

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Negril-Beach.jpg/250px-Negril-Beach.jpg)](/wiki/File:Negril-Beach.jpg)

A beach in [Negril](/wiki/Negril "Negril") with a hotel and restaurant

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/JamesBondBeach2.jpg/250px-JamesBondBeach2.jpg)](/wiki/File:JamesBondBeach2.jpg)

James Bond Beach in [Oracabessa](/wiki/Oracabessa "Oracabessa")

Tourism, which is the largest foreign exchange earner, showed improvement as well. In 1999 the total visitor arrivals was 2 million, an increase of 100,000 from the previous year.[[206]](#cite_note-208) Since 2017, Jamaica's tourism has risen exponentially, rising to 4.3 million average tourists per year. Jamaica's largest tourist markets are from North America, South America, and Europe. In 2017, Jamaica recorded a 91.3% increase in stopover visitors from Southern and Western Europe (and a 41% increase in stopover arrivals from January to September 2017 over the same period from the previous year) with Germany, Portugal and Spain registering the highest percentage gains.[[207]](#cite_note-209) In 2018, Jamaica won several World Travel Awards in Portugal winning the "Chairman's Award for Global Tourism Innovation", "Best Tourist Board in the Caribbean" "Best Honeymoon Destination", "Best Culinary Destination", "World's Leading Beach Destination" and "World's Leading Cruise Destination".[[208]](#cite_note-Jamaica_sweeps_World_Travel_Awards-210)[[209]](#cite_note-Jamaica_Ministry_of_Tourism-211) Two months later, the Travvy Tourism Awards held in New York City, awarded Jamaica's Tourism Minister Edmund Bartlett, with the inaugural Chairman's Award for, "Global Tourism Innovation for the Development of the Global Tourism Resilience and Crisis Management Centre (GTRCM)". Bartlett has also won the Pacific Travel Writer's Association's award in Germany for the, "2018 Best Tourism Minister of the Year".[[208]](#cite_note-Jamaica_sweeps_World_Travel_Awards-210)[[209]](#cite_note-Jamaica_Ministry_of_Tourism-211)[[210]](#cite_note-212)

### Industries

Jamaica's agricultural exports are [sugar](/wiki/Sugar "Sugar"), [bananas](/wiki/Bananas "Bananas"), [cocoa](/wiki/Cocoa_bean "Cocoa bean"),[[211]](#cite_note-213) [coconut](/wiki/Coconuts "Coconuts"), [molasses](/wiki/Molasses "Molasses")[[212]](#cite_note-EBJE-214) [oranges](/wiki/Orange_(fruit) "Orange (fruit)"), [limes](/wiki/Lime_(fruit) "Lime (fruit)"), [grapefruit](/wiki/Grapefruit "Grapefruit"),[[213]](#cite_note-215) [rum](/wiki/Rum "Rum"), [yams](/wiki/Yam_(vegetable) "Yam (vegetable)"), [allspice](/wiki/Allspice "Allspice") (of which it is the world's largest and "most exceptional quality" exporter),[[214]](#cite_note-216) and [Blue Mountain Coffee](/wiki/Blue_Mountain_Coffee "Blue Mountain Coffee") which is considered a world renowned gourmet brand.[[25]](#cite_note-EBJH-27)

Jamaica has a wide variety of industrial and commercial activities. The [aviation](/wiki/Aviation "Aviation") industry is able to perform most routine aircraft maintenance, except for heavy structural repairs. There is a considerable amount of technical support for transport and agricultural aviation. Jamaica has a considerable amount of [industrial engineering](/wiki/Industrial_engineering "Industrial engineering"), [light manufacturing](/wiki/Light_manufacturing "Light manufacturing"), including [metal fabrication](/wiki/Metal_fabrication "Metal fabrication"), metal roofing, and furniture [manufacturing](/wiki/Manufacturing "Manufacturing"). Food and [beverage](/wiki/Beverage "Beverage") processing, [glassware](/wiki/Glassware "Glassware") manufacturing, [software](/wiki/Software "Software") and [data processing](/wiki/Data_processing "Data processing"), [printing](/wiki/Printing "Printing") and [publishing](/wiki/Publishing "Publishing"), [insurance](/wiki/Insurance "Insurance") underwriting, [music](/wiki/Music "Music") and recording, and advanced [education](/wiki/Education "Education") activities can be found in the larger urban areas. The Jamaican [construction industry](/wiki/Construction_industry "Construction industry") is entirely self-sufficient, with professional technical standards and guidance.[[215]](#cite_note-217)

### Science and technology

Main articles: [Science and technology in Jamaica](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_Jamaica "Science and technology in Jamaica") and [List of Jamaican inventions and discoveries](/wiki/List_of_Jamaican_inventions_and_discoveries "List of Jamaican inventions and discoveries")

The Science, Technology and Innovation (STI) sector is guided by the National Commission on Science and Technology (NCST) and the Scientific Research Council (SRC). Both are under the direction of the Ministry of Science, Energy, and Technology.

Since the 1990s, the government has set an agenda to push the development of science and technology in Jamaica. Despite some successes, such as the growth of the [nutraceutical](/wiki/Nutraceutical "Nutraceutical") industry, it has been difficult to translate the results into domestic technologies, products and services – largely because of national budgetary constraints. However, with Jamaica's improved fiscal space, coming out of its recent [IMF](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund") programme, the government has pledged to increase expenditure on research and development.[[216]](#cite_note-218)[[217]](#cite_note-219)

Jamaicans have made noteworthy scientific and medical contributions. Amongst these include the discovery of [kwashiorkor](/wiki/Kwashiorkor "Kwashiorkor"), the pioneer of treatments for [pediatric](/wiki/Pediatrics "Pediatrics") [sickle cell anemia](/wiki/Sickle_cell_disease "Sickle cell disease") and the invention of various [spacecraft](/wiki/Spacecraft "Spacecraft") support systems.

## Infrastructure

### Transport

Further information: [Transport in Jamaica](/wiki/Transport_in_Jamaica "Transport in Jamaica")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Spanish_Town_Interchange-_Highway_2000_East_West.jpg/250px-Spanish_Town_Interchange-_Highway_2000_East_West.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spanish_Town_Interchange-_Highway_2000_East_West.jpg)

The [Spanish Town](/wiki/Spanish_Town "Spanish Town") interchange on the East–West toll road, part of [Highway 2000](/wiki/Highway_2000_(Jamaica) "Highway 2000 (Jamaica)")

The [transport](/wiki/Transportation "Transportation") infrastructure in Jamaica consists of [roadways](/wiki/Roadway "Roadway"), [railways](/wiki/Railway "Railway") and air transport, with roadways forming the backbone of the island's internal transport system.[[85]](#cite_note-EBJ-87)

#### Roadways

Main article: [Roads in Jamaica](/wiki/Roads_in_Jamaica "Roads in Jamaica")

The Jamaican road network consists of almost 21,000 kilometres (13,000 mi) of roads, of which over 15,000 kilometres (9,300 mi) is paved.[[218]](#cite_note-WorldFactbook-220) The [Jamaican Government](/wiki/Government_of_Jamaica "Government of Jamaica") has, since the late 1990s and in cooperation with private investors, embarked on a campaign of infrastructural improvement projects, one of which includes the creation of a system of [freeways](/wiki/Freeway "Freeway"), the first such access-controlled roadways of their kind on the island, connecting the main population centres of the island. This project has so far seen the completion of 33 kilometres (21 mi) of freeway.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Railways

Main article: [Railways of Jamaica](/wiki/Railways_of_Jamaica "Railways of Jamaica")

Railways in Jamaica no longer enjoy the prominent position they once did, having been largely replaced by roadways as the primary means of transport. Of the 272 kilometres (169 mi) of railway found in Jamaica, only 57 kilometres (35 mi) remain in operation, currently used to transport bauxite.[[218]](#cite_note-WorldFactbook-220) On 13 April 2011, a limited passenger service was resumed between May Pen, Spanish Town and Linstead.[[219]](#cite_note-221)

#### Air transport

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Montego_Bay_plane_Photo_D_Ramey_Logan.jpg/250px-Montego_Bay_plane_Photo_D_Ramey_Logan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Montego_Bay_plane_Photo_D_Ramey_Logan.jpg)

A [US Airways](/wiki/US_Airways "US Airways") aircraft landing at Montego Bay (2013)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Norman_Manley_International_Airport.jpg/250px-Norman_Manley_International_Airport.jpg)](/wiki/File:Norman_Manley_International_Airport.jpg)

Norman Manley International Airport

There are three international airports in Jamaica with modern [terminals](/wiki/Airport_terminal "Airport terminal"), long [runways](/wiki/Runway "Runway"), and the navigational equipment required to accommodate the large [jet aircraft](/wiki/Jet_aircraft "Jet aircraft") used in modern and [air travel](/wiki/Air_travel "Air travel"): [Norman Manley International Airport](/wiki/Norman_Manley_International_Airport "Norman Manley International Airport") in [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_Jamaica "Kingston, Jamaica"); [Ian Fleming International Airport](/wiki/Ian_Fleming_International_Airport "Ian Fleming International Airport") in [Boscobel](/wiki/Boscobel,_Jamaica "Boscobel, Jamaica"), [Saint Mary Parish](/wiki/Saint_Mary_Parish,_Jamaica "Saint Mary Parish, Jamaica"); and the island's largest and busiest airport, [Sir Donald Sangster International Airport](/wiki/Sir_Donald_Sangster_International_Airport "Sir Donald Sangster International Airport") in the [resort](/wiki/Resort "Resort") city of [Montego Bay](/wiki/Montego_Bay "Montego Bay"). Manley and Sangster International airports are home to the country's national airline, [Caribbean Airlines](/wiki/Caribbean_Airlines "Caribbean Airlines"). In addition there are local commuter airports at [Tinson Pen (Kingston)](/wiki/Tinson_Pen_Aerodrome "Tinson Pen Aerodrome"), [Port Antonio](/wiki/Port_Antonio "Port Antonio"), and [Negril](/wiki/Negril "Negril"), which cater to internal flights only. Many other small, rural centres are served by private airstrips on sugar estates or bauxite mines.[[85]](#cite_note-EBJ-87)

#### Ports, shipping and lighthouses

See also: [Lighthouses in Jamaica](/wiki/Lighthouses_in_Jamaica "Lighthouses in Jamaica")

Owing to its location in the Caribbean Sea in the [shipping lane](/wiki/Sea_lane "Sea lane") to the [Panama Canal](/wiki/Panama_Canal "Panama Canal") and relative proximity to large markets in North America and [emerging markets](/wiki/Emerging_market "Emerging market") in Latin America, Jamaica receives much traffic of [shipping containers](/wiki/Containerization "Containerization"). The [container terminal](/wiki/Container_terminal "Container terminal") at the Port of Kingston has undergone large expansion in capacity in recent years to handle growth both already realised as well as that which is projected in coming years.[[220]](#cite_note-nameJamaicaObserver-222) Montego Freeport in [Montego Bay](/wiki/Montego_Bay "Montego Bay") also handles a variety of cargo like (though more limited than) the Port of Kingston, mainly agricultural products.

There are several other ports positioned around the island, including Port Esquivel in [St. Catherine](/wiki/Saint_Catherine_Parish,_Jamaica "Saint Catherine Parish, Jamaica") ([WINDALCO](/wiki/WINDALCO "WINDALCO")), Rocky Point in [Clarendon](/wiki/Clarendon_Parish,_Jamaica "Clarendon Parish, Jamaica"), Port Kaiser in [St. Elizabeth](/wiki/Saint_Elizabeth_Parish,_Jamaica "Saint Elizabeth Parish, Jamaica"), Port Rhoades in Discovery Bay, Reynolds Pier in [Ocho Rios](/wiki/Ocho_Rios "Ocho Rios"), and Boundbrook Port in [Port Antonio](/wiki/Port_Antonio "Port Antonio").

To aid the navigation of shipping, Jamaica operates nine lighthouses.[[221]](#cite_note-ATSR-223) They are maintained by the Port Authority of Jamaica,[[222]](#cite_note-224) an agency of the Ministry of Transport and Works.[[223]](#cite_note-225)[[221]](#cite_note-ATSR-223)

### Energy

See also: [Solar power in Jamaica](/wiki/Solar_power_in_Jamaica "Solar power in Jamaica")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Jamaica_electricity_production.svg/250px-Jamaica_electricity_production.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Jamaica_electricity_production.svg)

Jamaica electricity production by source

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Jamaica_renewable_electricity_production.svg/250px-Jamaica_renewable_electricity_production.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Jamaica_renewable_electricity_production.svg)

Jamaica renewable electricity production by source

Jamaica depends on petroleum imports to satisfy its national energy needs.[[218]](#cite_note-WorldFactbook-220) Many test sites have been explored for oil, but no commercially viable quantities have been found.[[224]](#cite_note-pcj.com-226) The most convenient sources of imported oil and motor fuels (diesel, gasoline, and jet fuel) are from [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") and [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela").

Jamaica's electrical power is produced by diesel ([bunker oil](/wiki/Bunker_fuel "Bunker fuel")) generators located in [Old Harbour](/wiki/Old_Harbour,_Jamaica "Old Harbour, Jamaica"). This facility has been further equipped with liquid natural gas capability and storage. Other smaller power stations (most owned by the Jamaica Public Service Company,[[225]](#cite_note-227) the island's electricity provider) support the island's electrical grid including the Hunts Bay Power Station, the Bogue Power Station [Saint James](/wiki/Saint_James_Parish,_Jamaica "Saint James Parish, Jamaica"), the Rockfort Power Station [Saint Andrew](/wiki/Saint_Andrew_Parish,_Jamaica "Saint Andrew Parish, Jamaica") and small hydroelectric plants on the White River, Rio Bueno, Morant River, Black River (Maggotty) and Roaring River.[[226]](#cite_note-228) A wind farm, owned by the Petroleum Corporation of Jamaica, was established at Wigton, [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester_Parish "Manchester Parish").[[227]](#cite_note-229)

Jamaica has successfully operated a [SLOWPOKE-2 nuclear reactor](/wiki/SLOWPOKE_reactor "SLOWPOKE reactor") of 20 kW capacity since the early 1980s.[[228]](#cite_note-230)
In 2024, the Government committed to adding [small modular reactors](/wiki/Small_modular_reactors "Small modular reactors") (SMR) to the country's energy mix, signing a [memorandum of understanding](/wiki/Memorandum_of_understanding "Memorandum of understanding") (MOU) with [Atomic Energy of Canada Limited](/wiki/Atomic_Energy_of_Canada_Limited "Atomic Energy of Canada Limited") (AECL) and [Canadian Nuclear Laboratories](/wiki/Canadian_Nuclear_Laboratories_Research_Facilities "Canadian Nuclear Laboratories Research Facilities") to promote the adoption of nuclear power in Jamaica.[[229]](#cite_note-231)

Jamaica imports approximately 80,000 barrels (13,000 m3) of oil energy products per day,[[224]](#cite_note-pcj.com-226) including asphalt and lubrication products. Just 20% of imported fuels are used for road transportation, the rest being used by the bauxite industry, electricity generation, and aviation. 30,000 barrels/day of crude imports are processed into various motor fuels and asphalt by the Petrojam Refinery in Kingston.[[230]](#cite_note-232)

Jamaica produces enormous quantities of [drinking alcohol](/wiki/Hydrous_ethanol "Hydrous ethanol") (at least 5% water content), most of which appears to be consumed as beverages, and none used as motor fuel. Facilities exist to refine hydrous ethanol feedstock into [anhydrous ethanol](/wiki/Absolute_ethanol "Absolute ethanol") (0% water content), but as of 2007, the process appeared to be uneconomic and the production plant was idle.[[231]](#cite_note-233) The facility has since been purchased by West Indies Petroleum Ltd. and repurposed for petroleum distillates.

### Communication

Main article: [Telecommunications in Jamaica](/wiki/Telecommunications_in_Jamaica "Telecommunications in Jamaica")

Jamaica has a fully [digital telephone communication system](/wiki/Digital_telephony "Digital telephony") with a mobile penetration of over 95%.[[232]](#cite_note-EIU-234)

The country's two mobile operators – [FLOW Jamaica](/wiki/Flow_(brand) "Flow (brand)") (formerly LIME, bMobile and Cable and Wireless Jamaica) and [Digicel Jamaica](/wiki/Digicel "Digicel") have spent millions in network upgrades and expansion. The newest operator, Digicel was granted a licence in 2001 to operate mobile services in the newly liberalised telecom market that had once been the sole domain of the incumbent FLOW (then Cable and Wireless Jamaica) monopoly. Digicel opted for the more widely used [GSM](/wiki/GSM "GSM") wireless system, while a past operator, Oceanic (which became Claro Jamaica and later merged with Digicel Jamaica in 2011) opted for the [CDMA](/wiki/CDMA "CDMA") standard. FLOW (formerly "LIME" – pre-[Columbus Communications](/wiki/Columbus_Communications "Columbus Communications") merger) which had begun with [TDMA](/wiki/Digital_AMPS "Digital AMPS") standard, subsequently upgraded to GSM in 2002, decommissioned TDMA in 2006 and only utilised that standard until 2009 when LIME launched its 3G network.[[233]](#cite_note-235) Both operators currently provide islandwide coverage with HSPA+ (3G) technology. Currently, only [Digicel](/wiki/Digicel "Digicel") offers LTE to its customers[[234]](#cite_note-236) whereas FLOW Jamaica has committed to launching LTE in the cities of Kingston and Montego Bay, places where Digicel's LTE network is currently only found in, in short order.

A new entrant to the Jamaican communications market, [Flow Jamaica](/wiki/Columbus_Communications "Columbus Communications"), laid a new [submarine cable](/wiki/Submarine_communications_cable "Submarine communications cable") connecting Jamaica to the United States. This new cable increases the total number of submarine cables connecting Jamaica to the rest of the world to four. Cable and Wireless Communications (parent company of LIME) acquired the company in late 2014 and replaced their brand LIME with FLOW.[[235]](#cite_note-237) [FLOW Jamaica](/wiki/Flow_(brand) "Flow (brand)") currently has the most broadband and cable subscribers on the island and also has 1 million mobile subscribers,[[236]](#cite_note-238) second to Digicel (which had, at its peak, over 2 million mobile subscriptions on its network).

Digicel entered the broadband market in 2010 by offering WiMAX broadband,[[237]](#cite_note-239) capable of up to 6 Mbit/s per subscriber. To further their broadband share post-LIME/FLOW merger in 2014, the company introduced a new broadband service called Digicel Play,[[238]](#cite_note-240) which is Jamaica's second [FTTH](/wiki/FTTx "FTTx") offering (after LIME's deployment in selected communities in 2011[[239]](#cite_note-241)). It is currently only available in the parishes of Kingston, Portmore and St. Andrew. It offers speeds of up to 200 Mbit/s down, 100 Mbit/s up via a pure fibre optic network. Digicel's competitor, [FLOW Jamaica](/wiki/Flow_(brand) "Flow (brand)"), has a network consisting of [ADSL](/wiki/ADSL "ADSL"), Coaxial and Fibre to the Home (inherited from LIME) and only offers speeds up to 100 Mbit/s. FLOW has committed to expanding its Fibre offering to more areas in order to combat Digicel's entrance into the market.

It was announced that the Office and Utilities Regulations (OUR), Ministry of Science, Technology, Energy and Mining (MSTEM) and the Spectrum Management Authority (SMA) have given approval for another mobile operator licence in January 2016.[[240]](#cite_note-242) The identity of this entrant was ascertained on 20 May 2016, when the Jamaican Government named the new carrier as Symbiote Investments Limited operating under the name [Caricel](/wiki/Caricel "Caricel").[[241]](#cite_note-243) The company will focus on 4G LTE data offerings and will first go live in the Kingston Metropolitan Area and will expand to the rest of Jamaica thereafter.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Jamaica](/wiki/Culture_of_Jamaica "Culture of Jamaica")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Bob-Marley-in-Concert_Zurich_05-30-80.jpg/250px-Bob-Marley-in-Concert_Zurich_05-30-80.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bob-Marley-in-Concert_Zurich_05-30-80.jpg)

[Bob Marley](/wiki/Bob_Marley "Bob Marley"), one of the most famous reggae artists from Jamaica

### Music

Main article: [Music of Jamaica](/wiki/Music_of_Jamaica "Music of Jamaica")

Jamaican culture has a strong global presence. The musical genres [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae"), [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska"), [mento](/wiki/Mento "Mento"), [rocksteady](/wiki/Rocksteady "Rocksteady"), [dub](/wiki/Dub_music "Dub music"), and, more recently, [dancehall](/wiki/Dancehall "Dancehall") and [ragga](/wiki/Ragga "Ragga") all originated in the island's vibrant, popular urban recording industry.[[242]](#cite_note-244) These have themselves gone on to influence numerous other genres, such as [punk rock](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock") (through reggae and ska), [dub poetry](/wiki/Dub_poetry "Dub poetry"), [New Wave](/wiki/New_wave_music "New wave music"), [two-tone](/wiki/2_Tone_(music_genre) "2 Tone (music genre)"), [lovers rock](/wiki/Lovers_rock "Lovers rock"), [reggaeton](/wiki/Reggaeton "Reggaeton"), [jungle](/wiki/Oldschool_jungle "Oldschool jungle"), [drum and bass](/wiki/Drum_and_bass "Drum and bass"), [dubstep](/wiki/Dubstep "Dubstep"), [grime](/wiki/Grime_(music_genre) "Grime (music genre)") and American [rap](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music") music. Some rappers, such as [The Notorious B.I.G.](/wiki/The_Notorious_B.I.G. "The Notorious B.I.G."), [Busta Rhymes](/wiki/Busta_Rhymes "Busta Rhymes"), and [Heavy D](/wiki/Heavy_D "Heavy D"), are of Jamaican descent.

[Bob Marley](/wiki/Bob_Marley "Bob Marley") is probably the best known Jamaican musician; with his band [the Wailers](/wiki/The_Wailers "The Wailers") he had a string of hits in 1960s–70s, popularising reggae internationally and going on to sell millions of records.[[243]](#cite_note-245)[[244]](#cite_note-Toynbee2013-246) Many other internationally known artists were born in Jamaica, including [Toots Hibbert](/wiki/Toots_Hibbert "Toots Hibbert"), [Millie Small](/wiki/Millie_Small "Millie Small"), [Lee "Scratch" Perry](/wiki/Lee_%22Scratch%22_Perry "Lee \"Scratch\" Perry"), [Gregory Isaacs](/wiki/Gregory_Isaacs "Gregory Isaacs"), [Half Pint](/wiki/Half_Pint "Half Pint"), [Protoje](/wiki/Protoje "Protoje"), [Peter Tosh](/wiki/Peter_Tosh "Peter Tosh"), [Bunny Wailer](/wiki/Bunny_Wailer "Bunny Wailer"), [Big Youth](/wiki/Big_Youth "Big Youth"), [Jimmy Cliff](/wiki/Jimmy_Cliff "Jimmy Cliff"), [Dennis Brown](/wiki/Dennis_Brown "Dennis Brown"), [Desmond Dekker](/wiki/Desmond_Dekker "Desmond Dekker"), [Beres Hammond](/wiki/Beres_Hammond "Beres Hammond"), [Beenie Man](/wiki/Beenie_Man "Beenie Man"), [Shaggy](/wiki/Shaggy_(musician) "Shaggy (musician)"), [Grace Jones](/wiki/Grace_Jones "Grace Jones"), [Shabba Ranks](/wiki/Shabba_Ranks "Shabba Ranks"), [Super Cat](/wiki/Super_Cat "Super Cat"), [Buju Banton](/wiki/Buju_Banton "Buju Banton"), [Sean Paul](/wiki/Sean_Paul "Sean Paul"), [I Wayne](/wiki/I_Wayne "I Wayne"), [Bounty Killer](/wiki/Bounty_Killer "Bounty Killer") and many others. Bands that came from Jamaica include [Black Uhuru](/wiki/Black_Uhuru "Black Uhuru"), [Third World Band](/wiki/Third_World_Band "Third World Band"), [Inner Circle](/wiki/Inner_Circle_(reggae_band) "Inner Circle (reggae band)"), [Chalice Reggae Band](/wiki/Chalice_Reggae_Band "Chalice Reggae Band"), [Culture](/wiki/Culture_(band) "Culture (band)"), Fab Five and [Morgan Heritage](/wiki/Morgan_Heritage "Morgan Heritage").

### Literature

Main article: [Jamaican literature](/wiki/Jamaican_literature "Jamaican literature")

The journalist and author [H. G. de Lisser](/wiki/H._G._de_Lisser "H. G. de Lisser") used his native country as the setting for his many novels.[[245]](#cite_note-Hughes-247) Born in [Falmouth, Jamaica](/wiki/Falmouth,_Jamaica "Falmouth, Jamaica"), de Lisser worked as a reporter for the *[Jamaica Times](/wiki/Jamaica_Times "Jamaica Times")* at a young age and in 1920 began publishing the magazine *[Planters' Punch](/wiki/Planters%27_Punch "Planters' Punch")*. *The White Witch of Rosehall* is one of his better-known novels. He was named Honorary President of the Jamaican Press Association; he worked throughout his professional career to promote the Jamaican sugar industry. [Roger Mais](/wiki/Roger_Mais "Roger Mais"), a journalist, poet, and playwright wrote many short stories, plays, and novels, including *The Hills Were Joyful Together* (1953), *Brother Man* (1954), and *Black Lightning* (1955).[[246]](#cite_note-Hawthorne_7-248)

[Ian Fleming](/wiki/Ian_Fleming "Ian Fleming"), who had a home in Jamaica where he spent considerable time, repeatedly used the island as a setting in his [James Bond](/wiki/James_Bond "James Bond") novels, including *[Live and Let Die](/wiki/Live_and_Let_Die_(novel) "Live and Let Die (novel)")*, *[Doctor No](/wiki/Dr._No_(novel) "Dr. No (novel)")*, "[For Your Eyes Only](/wiki/For_Your_Eyes_Only_(short_story) "For Your Eyes Only (short story)")", *[The Man with the Golden Gun](/wiki/The_Man_with_the_Golden_Gun_(novel) "The Man with the Golden Gun (novel)")*, and *[Octopussy and The Living Daylights](/wiki/Octopussy_and_The_Living_Daylights "Octopussy and The Living Daylights")*.[[247]](#cite_note-249) [Marlon James](/wiki/Marlon_James_(novelist) "Marlon James (novelist)") (1970), novelist has published three novels: *John Crow's Devil* (2005), *[The Book of Night Women](/wiki/The_Book_of_Night_Women "The Book of Night Women")* (2009) and *[A Brief History of Seven Killings](/wiki/A_Brief_History_of_Seven_Killings "A Brief History of Seven Killings")* (2014), winner of the 2015 [Man Booker Prize](/wiki/Man_Booker_Prize "Man Booker Prize").[[248]](#cite_note-250)

### Film

See also: [List of Jamaican films](/wiki/List_of_Jamaican_films "List of Jamaican films")

Jamaica has a history in the film industry dating from the early 1960s. A look at delinquent youth in Jamaica is presented in the 1970s musical crime film *[The Harder They Come](/wiki/The_Harder_They_Come "The Harder They Come")*, starring [Jimmy Cliff](/wiki/Jimmy_Cliff "Jimmy Cliff") as a frustrated (and [psychopathic](/wiki/Psychopathic "Psychopathic")) reggae musician who descends into a murderous crime spree.[[249]](#cite_note-251) Other notable Jamaican films include *[Countryman](/wiki/Countryman_(film) "Countryman (film)")*, *[Rockers](/wiki/Rockers_(1978_film) "Rockers (1978 film)")*, *[Dancehall Queen](/wiki/Dancehall_Queen "Dancehall Queen")*, *[One Love](/wiki/One_Love_(2003_film) "One Love (2003 film)")*, *[Shottas](/wiki/Shottas "Shottas")*, *[Out the Gate](/wiki/Out_the_Gate_(film) "Out the Gate (film)")*, *[Third World Cop](/wiki/Third_World_Cop "Third World Cop")* and *[Kingston Paradise](/wiki/Kingston_Paradise "Kingston Paradise")*. Jamaica is also often used as a filming location, such as the James Bond film *[Dr. No](/wiki/Dr._No_(film) "Dr. No (film)")* (1962), [*Papillon*](/wiki/Papillon_(1973_film) "Papillon (1973 film)") (1973) starring [Steve McQueen](/wiki/Steve_McQueen "Steve McQueen"), *[Cocktail](/wiki/Cocktail_(1988_film) "Cocktail (1988 film)")* (1988) starring [Tom Cruise](/wiki/Tom_Cruise "Tom Cruise"), and the 1993 Disney comedy *[Cool Runnings](/wiki/Cool_Runnings "Cool Runnings")*, which is loosely based on the true story of Jamaica's first bobsled team trying to make it in the Winter Olympics.

### Cuisine

Main article: [Jamaican cuisine](/wiki/Jamaican_cuisine "Jamaican cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Curry_Goat_with_rice_and_peas_%28in_this_case_kidney_beans%29.jpg/250px-Curry_Goat_with_rice_and_peas_%28in_this_case_kidney_beans%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Curry_Goat_with_rice_and_peas_(in_this_case_kidney_beans).jpg)

Jamaican [curry goat](/wiki/Curry_goat "Curry goat") with [rice and peas](/wiki/Rice_and_peas "Rice and peas")

The island is famous for its [Jamaican jerk spice](/wiki/Jamaican_jerk_spice "Jamaican jerk spice"), [curries](/wiki/Curry "Curry") and [rice and peas](/wiki/Rice_and_peas "Rice and peas") which is integral to Jamaican cuisine. Jamaica is also home to [Red Stripe](/wiki/Red_Stripe "Red Stripe") beer and [Jamaican Blue Mountain Coffee](/wiki/Jamaican_Blue_Mountain_Coffee "Jamaican Blue Mountain Coffee").

### Sport

Main article: [Sport in Jamaica](/wiki/Sport_in_Jamaica "Sport in Jamaica")

Sport is an integral part of national life in Jamaica and the island's athletes tend to perform to a standard well above what might ordinarily be expected of such a small country.[[13]](#cite_note-track-14) While the most popular local sport is [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"), on the international stage Jamaicans have tended to do particularly well at [track and field athletics](/wiki/Track_and_field_athletics "Track and field athletics").[[13]](#cite_note-track-14)[[250]](#cite_note-jamsports-252)

The country was one of the venues of [2007 Cricket World Cup](/wiki/2007_Cricket_World_Cup "2007 Cricket World Cup") and the [West Indies cricket team](/wiki/West_Indies_cricket_team "West Indies cricket team") is one of 12 [ICC](/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council") full member teams that participate in international [Test cricket](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket").[[251]](#cite_note-cricket-253) The [Jamaica national cricket team](/wiki/Jamaica_national_cricket_team "Jamaica national cricket team") competes regionally, and also provides players for the West Indies team. [Sabina Park](/wiki/Sabina_Park "Sabina Park") is the only Test venue in the island, but the [Greenfield Stadium](/wiki/Greenfield_Stadium_(Trelawny) "Greenfield Stadium (Trelawny)") is also used for cricket.[[252]](#cite_note-cricketgrounds-254)[[253]](#cite_note-Greenfield-255)

Since independence, Jamaica has consistently produced world-class athletes in track and field.[[13]](#cite_note-track-14) Over the past six decades Jamaica has produced dozens of world-class sprinters, including Olympic and world champion [Usain Bolt](/wiki/Usain_Bolt "Usain Bolt"), world record holder in the 100 m for men at 9.58s, and 200 m for men at 19.19s. Other noteworthy Jamaican sprinters are [Arthur Wint](/wiki/Arthur_Wint "Arthur Wint"), the first Jamaican Olympic gold medalist; [Donald Quarrie](/wiki/Donald_Quarrie "Donald Quarrie"), [Elaine Thompson](/wiki/Elaine_Thompson "Elaine Thompson") double Olympic champion from Rio 2016 in the 100 m and 200 m, Olympic champion and former 200 m world record holder; [Roy Anthony Bridge](/wiki/Roy_Anthony_Bridge "Roy Anthony Bridge"), part of the International Olympic Committee; [Merlene Ottey](/wiki/Merlene_Ottey "Merlene Ottey"); [Delloreen Ennis-London](/wiki/Delloreen_Ennis-London "Delloreen Ennis-London"); [Shelly-Ann Fraser-Pryce](/wiki/Shelly-Ann_Fraser "Shelly-Ann Fraser"), the former world and two-time Olympic 100 m champion; [Kerron Stewart](/wiki/Kerron_Stewart "Kerron Stewart"); [Aleen Bailey](/wiki/Aleen_Bailey "Aleen Bailey"); [Juliet Cuthbert](/wiki/Juliet_Cuthbert "Juliet Cuthbert"); three-time Olympic gold medalist; [Veronica Campbell-Brown](/wiki/Veronica_Campbell-Brown "Veronica Campbell-Brown"); [Sherone Simpson](/wiki/Sherone_Simpson "Sherone Simpson"); [Brigitte Foster-Hylton](/wiki/Brigitte_Foster-Hylton "Brigitte Foster-Hylton"); [Yohan Blake](/wiki/Yohan_Blake "Yohan Blake"); [Herb McKenley](/wiki/Herb_McKenley "Herb McKenley"); [George Rhoden](/wiki/George_Rhoden "George Rhoden"), Olympic gold medalist; [Deon Hemmings](/wiki/Deon_Hemmings "Deon Hemmings"), Olympic gold medalist; as well as [Asafa Powell](/wiki/Asafa_Powell "Asafa Powell"), former 100 m world record holder and two-time 100 m Olympic finalist and gold medal winner in the men's 2008 Olympic 4 × 100 m. American Olympic winner [Sanya Richards-Ross](/wiki/Sanya_Richards-Ross "Sanya Richards-Ross") was also born in Jamaica.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Usain_Bolt_after_200_m_final_Beijing_2015.jpg/250px-Usain_Bolt_after_200_m_final_Beijing_2015.jpg)](/wiki/File:Usain_Bolt_after_200_m_final_Beijing_2015.jpg)

[Usain Bolt](/wiki/Usain_Bolt "Usain Bolt") is widely considered the greatest sprinter of all time.

[Association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") and [horse-racing](/wiki/Horse-racing "Horse-racing") are other popular sports in Jamaica. The [national football team](/wiki/Jamaica_National_Football_Team "Jamaica National Football Team") qualified for the [1998](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup "1998 FIFA World Cup") [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup"). Horse racing was Jamaica's first sport. Today, horse racing provides jobs for about 20,000 people including horse breeders, groomers, and trainers. Also, several Jamaicans are known internationally for their success in horse racing including Richard DePass, who once held the Guinness Book of World Records for the most wins in a day, Canadian awards winner George HoSang, and American award winners Charlie Hussey, Andrew Ramgeet, and Barrington Harvey.[[254]](#cite_note-256)

Race car driving is also a popular sport in Jamaica with several car racing tracks and racing associations across the country.[[255]](#cite_note-257)

The [Jamaica national bobsled team](/wiki/Jamaica_national_bobsled_team "Jamaica national bobsled team") was once a serious contender in the [Winter Olympics](/wiki/Winter_Olympics "Winter Olympics"), beating many well-established teams. Chess and basketball are widely played in Jamaica and are supported by the Jamaica Chess Federation (JCF) and the Jamaica Basketball Federation (JBF), respectively. [Netball](/wiki/Netball "Netball") is also very popular on the island, with the [Jamaica national netball team](/wiki/Jamaica_national_netball_team "Jamaica national netball team") called The Sunshine Girls consistently ranking in the top five in the world.[[256]](#cite_note-258)

[Rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") has been played in Jamaica since 2006.[[257]](#cite_note-259)
The [Jamaica national rugby league team](/wiki/Jamaica_national_rugby_league_team "Jamaica national rugby league team") is made up of players who play in Jamaica and from [UK](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") based professional and semi professional clubs (notably in the [Super League](/wiki/Super_League "Super League") and [Championship](/wiki/Championship_(rugby_league) "Championship (rugby league)")). In November 2018 for the first time ever, the Jamaican rugby league team qualified for the [Rugby League World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_League_World_Cup "Rugby League World Cup") after defeating the [USA](/wiki/United_States_national_rugby_league_team "United States national rugby league team") and [Canada](/wiki/Canada_national_rugby_league_team "Canada national rugby league team"). Jamaica will play in the [2021 Rugby League World Cup](/wiki/2021_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2021 Rugby League World Cup") in England.[[258]](#cite_note-260)

According to [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN"), the highest paid Jamaican professional athlete in 2011 was [Justin Masterson](/wiki/Justin_Masterson "Justin Masterson"), starting pitcher for the baseball team [Cleveland Indians](/wiki/Cleveland_Indians "Cleveland Indians") in the United States.[[259]](#cite_note-261)
